ACK_ANSWER_REPORT.md:3 advice ACK ‚Äî Acknowledgment of mathematician's answer :: Date: 2026-04-26
ACK_ANSWER_REPORT.md:7 advice Summary of the answer :: The mathematician's `ANSWER_REPORT.md` confirms that **the LITERAL `Sd^N` refinement theorem we were trying to prove is FALSE**, provides a clean 1-dimensional counterexample, and prescribes a replacement strategy via the classical PL-topology overlay-cell-complex construction.
ACK_ANSWER_REPORT.md:13 advice 1. The original theorem is genuinely false :: > **Counterexample (1-dimensional):** Take `K = [0,1]` (a single 1-simplex). Take `K' =` the subdivision of `[0,1]` at `1/3` (so `K' = {[0, 1/3], [1/3, 1]}` plus their faces). > > Iterated barycentric subdivisions of `[0,1]` only produce vertices at **dyadic** points `m/2^k`. The point `1/3` is never a vertex of `Sd^N K` for any `N`. > > So for every `N`, there's a sub-interval of `Sd^N K` that contains `1/3` strictly in its interior. That sub-interval is NOT contained in `[0, 1/3]` (would need its right endpoint ‚â§ 1/3) and NOT contained in `[1/3, 1]` (would need its left endpoint ‚â• 1/3). It crosses the boundary. > > Therefore `Sd^N K` does NOT refine `K'` literally for any `N`.
ACK_ANSWER_REPORT.md:21 advice 1. The original theorem is genuinely false :: This is conclusive. I should have constructed this counterexample weeks ago ‚Äî it's a textbook elementary example. The previous attempts (rel-distance bounds, K'-carrier nesting) were dead-ends because the underlying claim was false.
ACK_ANSWER_REPORT.md:25 advice 2. The replacement strategy is well-known PL topology :: Instead of trying to construct a common subdivision via iterated barycentric subdivision, use the **overlay construction**:
ACK_ANSWER_REPORT.md:27 advice 2. The replacement strategy is well-known PL topology :: ```text Given finite simplicial complexes L1, L2 with |L1| = |L2|: 1. Form the raw overlay cells {œÉ ‚à© œÑ : œÉ ‚àà L1, œÑ ‚àà L2, œÉ ‚à© œÑ ‚âÝ ‚àÖ}. 2. Close under faces to get a finite convex cell complex C. 3. Triangulate C (Hudson Lemma 1.4 OR order-complex triangulation). 4. The triangulation M is the common subdivision: every simplex of M is contained in œÉ ‚à© œÑ for some œÉ ‚àà L1 and œÑ ‚àà L2 (literal containment). ```
ACK_ANSWER_REPORT.md:36 advice 2. The replacement strategy is well-known PL topology :: This avoids the dyadic-vs-irrational issue entirely because the overlay construction creates new vertices AT the boundaries of both L1 and L2 (e.g., at `1/3` in the counterexample).

ANSWER_REPORT.md:5 advice Executive summary :: The original auxiliary theorem
ANSWER_REPORT.md:7 advice Executive summary :: ```text For finite Euclidean simplicial complexes K and K', if K' subdivides K, then some iterated barycentric subdivision Sd^N K literally refines K'. ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:12 advice Executive summary :: is false. A one-dimensional counterexample is enough: subdivide `[0,1]` at `1/3`. Every iterated barycentric subdivision of the original interval has dyadic vertices, so for every `N` exactly one dyadic subinterval crosses `1/3`; that subinterval is not contained in either simplex of the subdivision at `1/3`.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:14 advice Executive summary :: Therefore the existing `sorry` on the literal iterated barycentric refinement theorem should not be attacked further. The right replacement is the classical PL-topology construction of a common subdivision by overlaying the two complexes, forming a finite convex cell complex from pairwise intersections, and then triangulating that cell complex.

ANSWER_REPORT.md:30 advice Executive summary :: This path keeps the downstream PL-transport arguments intact, because the final common subdivision is literal: every simplex of `M` is contained in a simplex of `L1` and also in a simplex of `L2`.

ANSWER_REPORT.md:36 advice Relevant Hudson material to formalize :: Only a small fragment of Hudson is needed.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:40 advice Hudson definitions/facts needed :: Hudson introduces convex linear cells and cell complexes in Chapter I, Section 2. The needed facts are:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:42 advice Hudson definitions/facts needed :: 1. A cell is convex and is the convex hull of its vertices. 2. The intersection of cells is a cell. 3. The convex hull of a finite set is a cell. 4. A convex linear cell complex is a finite set of cells closed under faces, with pairwise intersections empty or common faces. 5. A subdivision of a cell complex has the same underlying polyhedron and every finer cell is contained in some coarser cell. 6. Lemma 1.4: every cell complex has a simplicial subdivision with no extra vertices.

ANSWER_REPORT.md:69 advice Central construction :: Given finite Euclidean simplicial complexes `L1` and `L2` with the same polyhedron, define the overlay cells:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:71 advice Central construction :: ```isabelle definition overlay_cells where "overlay_cells L1 L2 = {C. ‚àÉœÉ‚ààL1. ‚àÉœÑ‚ààL2. C = œÉ ‚à© œÑ ‚àß C ‚âÝ {}}" ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:77 advice Central construction :: Call this set `C`.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:79 advice Central construction :: Each `œÉ ‚à© œÑ` is generally not a simplex. It is a convex polytope/cell. The collection of all such intersections is the overlay cell complex.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:81 advice Central construction :: Then triangulate this cell complex. The resulting simplicial complex `M` is the desired common subdivision.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:83 advice Central construction :: Reason:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:85 advice Central construction :: ```text œÅ ‚àà M ‚áí œÅ lies in some overlay cell œÉ ‚à© œÑ ‚áí œÅ ‚äÜ œÉ and œÅ ‚äÜ œÑ ‚áí œÅ is contained in a simplex of L1 and in a simplex of L2. ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:92 advice Central construction :: The covering condition is inherited because the overlay cells cover the common polyhedron and the triangulation covers the overlay cells.

ANSWER_REPORT.md:119 advice 1. Define convex linear cells :: Use the most convenient representation for your current Isabelle library. There are two viable options.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:123 advice Option A: halfspace representation :: ```isabelle definition geotop_cell :: "'a::euclidean_space set ‚áí bool" where "geotop_cell C ‚ü∑ C ‚âÝ {} ‚àß compact C ‚àß convex C ‚àß (‚àÉF. finite F ‚àß C = ‚ãÇ F ‚àß (‚àÄH‚ààF. ‚àÉa b. H = {x. inner a x ‚â§ b}))" ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:131 advice Option A: halfspace representation :: Pros: - intersections are immediate; - faces are naturally exposed faces.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:135 advice Option A: halfspace representation :: Cons: - more work to connect with simplices if existing simplex theory is convex-hull based.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:140 advice Option B: polytope/convex-hull representation :: ```isabelle definition geotop_cell :: "'a::euclidean_space set ‚áí bool" where "geotop_cell C ‚ü∑ C ‚âÝ {} ‚àß (‚àÉV. finite V ‚àß C = convex hull V)" ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:146 advice Option B: polytope/convex-hull representation :: Pros: - easy to show every simplex is a cell; - probably closer to existing simplex formalization.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:150 advice Option B: polytope/convex-hull representation :: Cons: - intersection of polytopes needs a lemma: intersection of two convex hulls of finite sets is again a polytope.

ANSWER_REPORT.md:251 advice 7. Intersection of two cells is a face in a cell complex :: For an arbitrary pair of cells, `A ‚à© B` is not necessarily a face of `A`. For example, two squares can overlap in a smaller square inside one of them. But in the overlay situation, this is controlled by the simplicial-complex structure.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:253 advice 7. Intersection of two cells is a face in a cell complex :: The important lemma is not ‚Äúall cell intersections are faces.‚Äù The important lemma is:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:255 advice 7. Intersection of two cells is a face in a cell complex :: ```text For overlay cells C1 = œÉ1 ‚à© œÑ1 and C2 = œÉ2 ‚à© œÑ2, C1 ‚à© C2 = (œÉ1 ‚à© œÉ2) ‚à© (œÑ1 ‚à© œÑ2). Since L1 and L2 are simplicial complexes, œÉ1 ‚à© œÉ2 is empty or a common face, and œÑ1 ‚à© œÑ2 is empty or a common face. Therefore C1 ‚à© C2 is empty or a common face of C1 and C2. ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:263 advice 7. Intersection of two cells is a face in a cell complex :: This is the key bridge from simplicial intersection properties to cell-complex intersection properties.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:265 advice 7. Intersection of two cells is a face in a cell complex :: ---

A ‚à© B = {} ‚à® (geotop_cell_face (A ‚à© B) A ‚àß geotop_cell_face (A ‚à© B) B))" ```

ANSWER_REPORT.md:306 advice Phase 4 ‚Äî Overlay cell complex :: Let:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:308 advice Phase 4 ‚Äî Overlay cell complex :: ```isabelle definition geotop_overlay_cells where "geotop_overlay_cells L1 L2 = {C. ‚àÉœÉ‚ààL1. ‚àÉœÑ‚ààL2. C = œÉ ‚à© œÑ ‚àß C ‚âÝ {}}" ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:316 advice 11. Overlay finite :: ```isabelle lemma geotop_overlay_cells_finite: assumes "finite L1" "finite L2" shows "finite (geotop_overlay_cells L1 L2)" ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:322 advice 11. Overlay finite :: Proof: - `overlay_cells L1 L2` is an image/subset of `L1 √ó L2` under `(œÉ,œÑ) ‚Ü¶ œÉ ‚à© œÑ`.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:327 advice 12. Overlay cells are cells :: ```isabelle lemma geotop_overlay_cell_is_cell: assumes "geotop_simplicial_complex L1" "geotop_simplicial_complex L2" assumes "C ‚àà geotop_overlay_cells L1 L2" shows "geotop_cell C" ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:334 advice 12. Overlay cells are cells :: Proof: - obtain `œÉ‚ààL1`, `œÑ‚ààL2`, `C = œÉ ‚à© œÑ`, nonempty; - simplices are cells; - intersection of cells is a cell.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:341 advice 13. Overlay covers the common polyhedron :: ```isabelle lemma geotop_overlay_cells_union: assumes "geotop_polyhedron L1 = geotop_polyhedron L2" shows "‚ãÉ (geotop_overlay_cells L1 L2) = geotop_polyhedron L1" ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:347 advice 13. Overlay covers the common polyhedron :: Proof: - `‚äÜ`: every overlay cell is contained in some `œÉ‚ààL1`. - `‚äá`: if `x‚àà|L1|`, then `x‚ààœÉ` for some `œÉ‚ààL1`. Since `|L1|=|L2|`, also `x‚ààœÑ` for some `œÑ‚ààL2`. Hence `x‚ààœÉ‚à©œÑ`, and that intersection is a nonempty overlay cell.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:353 advice 14. Overlay refines left and right :: ```isabelle lemma geotop_overlay_refines_left: assumes "C ‚àà geotop_overlay_cells L1 L2" shows "‚àÉœÉ‚ààL1. C ‚äÜ œÉ"
ANSWER_REPORT.md:358 advice 14. Overlay refines left and right :: lemma geotop_overlay_refines_right: assumes "C ‚àà geotop_overlay_cells L1 L2" shows "‚àÉœÑ‚ààL2. C ‚äÜ œÑ" ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:363 advice 14. Overlay refines left and right :: Immediate from definition.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:367 advice 15. The hard lemma: faces of overlay cells are overlay cells :: This is the main subtlety if your cell-complex definition requires closure under **all** cell faces.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:369 advice 15. The hard lemma: faces of overlay cells are overlay cells :: Desired statement:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:371 advice 15. The hard lemma: faces of overlay cells are overlay cells :: ```isabelle lemma geotop_overlay_closed_under_faces: assumes "geotop_simplicial_complex L1" "geotop_simplicial_complex L2" assumes "C ‚àà geotop_overlay_cells L1 L2" assumes "geotop_cell_face F C" shows "F ‚àà geotop_overlay_cells L1 L2" ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:379 advice 15. The hard lemma: faces of overlay cells are overlay cells :: This can be difficult in full generality depending on the definition of `cell_face`. The direct geometric statement is:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:381 advice 15. The hard lemma: faces of overlay cells are overlay cells :: ```text If C = œÉ ‚à© œÑ and F is a face of C, then there exist faces œÉ' ‚â§ œÉ and œÑ' ‚â§ œÑ such that F = œÉ' ‚à© œÑ'. ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:386 advice 15. The hard lemma: faces of overlay cells are overlay cells :: This is true for convex polytopes defined by active inequalities: `œÉ` and `œÑ` are finite intersections of halfspaces. The cell `œÉ ‚à© œÑ` has facets inherited from active inequalities of either `œÉ` or `œÑ`. A face of `œÉ ‚à© œÑ` is obtained by turning some of those inequalities into equalities. Those inherited from `œÉ` define a face `œÉ'` of `œÉ`; those inherited from `œÑ` define a face `œÑ'` of `œÑ`; then `F = œÉ' ‚à© œÑ'`.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:390 advice Isabelle-friendly version :: If this lemma becomes too hard, use a weaker cell-complex definition where the cell set is not required to contain every face at first. Then close the overlay under faces:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:392 advice Isabelle-friendly version :: ```isabelle definition geotop_face_closure where "geotop_face_closure C = {F. ‚àÉA‚ààC. geotop_cell_face F A}" ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:397 advice Isabelle-friendly version :: Then prove:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:399 advice Isabelle-friendly version :: ```isabelle lemma finite_face_closure_overlay: assumes "finite L1" "finite L2" shows "finite (geotop_face_closure (geotop_overlay_cells L1 L2))" ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:405 advice Isabelle-friendly version :: This uses the fact that a cell has finitely many faces. It avoids proving immediately that every face of `œÉ ‚à© œÑ` is exactly another original pairwise intersection.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:407 advice Isabelle-friendly version :: Then use the face-closed set:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:409 advice Isabelle-friendly version :: ```isabelle C = geotop_face_closure (geotop_overlay_cells L1 L2) ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:413 advice Isabelle-friendly version :: This is often more robust. It adds extra cells, but each new face is still contained in its parent overlay cell, so it is still contained in some simplex of `L1` and some simplex of `L2`. This is enough for common subdivision.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:417 advice Strong recommendation :: Use **face closure**. It is safer for formalization.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:419 advice Strong recommendation :: Then the overlay definition becomes:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:421 advice Strong recommendation :: ```isabelle definition geotop_overlay_complex where "geotop_overlay_complex L1 L2 = geotop_face_closure (geotop_overlay_cells L1 L2)" ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:427 advice Strong recommendation :: Now the closure-under-faces property is by construction.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:429 advice Strong recommendation :: You will need:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:431 advice Strong recommendation :: ```isabelle lemma geotop_overlay_complex_finite: assumes "finite L1" "finite L2" shows "finite (geotop_overlay_complex L1 L2)" ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:437 advice Strong recommendation :: This depends on:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:439 advice Strong recommendation :: ```isabelle lemma geotop_cell_finite_faces: assumes "geotop_cell A" shows "finite {F. geotop_cell_face F A}" ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:445 advice Strong recommendation :: For polytopes/cells this is standard.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:449 advice 16. Overlay intersection property :: For the face-closed overlay complex, prove:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:451 advice 16. Overlay intersection property :: ```isabelle lemma geotop_overlay_complex_intersection_common_face: assumes "geotop_simplicial_complex L1" "geotop_simplicial_complex L2" assumes "A ‚àà geotop_overlay_complex L1 L2" assumes "B ‚àà geotop_overlay_complex L1 L2" shows "A ‚à© B = {} ‚à® (geotop_cell_face (A ‚à© B) A ‚àß geotop_cell_face (A ‚à© B) B)" ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:460 advice 16. Overlay intersection property :: If `A` and `B` are themselves faces of original overlay cells `œÉ1‚à©œÑ1` and `œÉ2‚à©œÑ2`, this follows because:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:462 advice 16. Overlay intersection property :: ```text A ‚â§ œÉ1‚à©œÑ1, B ‚â§ œÉ2‚à©œÑ2 A‚à©B is a face of (œÉ1‚à©œÑ1)‚à©(œÉ2‚à©œÑ2) (œÉ1‚à©œÉ2) and (œÑ1‚à©œÑ2) are faces or empty by simpliciality therefore the total intersection is a cell face of both A and B ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:469 advice 16. Overlay intersection property :: This may require a few convex-face lemmas:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:471 advice 16. Overlay intersection property :: ```isabelle lemma face_Int_face_of_intersection: assumes "geotop_cell_face A A0" "geotop_cell_face B B0" assumes "A ‚à© B ‚âÝ {}" shows "geotop_cell_face (A ‚à© B) (A0 ‚à© B0)" ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:478 advice 16. Overlay intersection property :: and related transitivity/subface lemmas.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:480 advice 16. Overlay intersection property :: If this remains difficult, a more direct alternative is to define the overlay complex using the **arrangement of all affine supporting hyperplanes** of all simplices in both complexes. Then cells are indexed by sign/equality patterns of finitely many inequalities. In such an arrangement, intersection and face properties are almost syntactic. This is more infrastructure, but sometimes easier for a proof assistant.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:484 advice 17. Overlay is a cell complex :: ```isabelle lemma geotop_overlay_complex_is_cell_complex: assumes "geotop_simplicial_complex L1" "geotop_simplicial_complex L2" assumes "finite L1" "finite L2" shows "geotop_cell_complex (geotop_overlay_complex L1 L2)" ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:491 advice 17. Overlay is a cell complex :: Use: - finite face closure; - every element is a cell; - closure under faces by definition; - pairwise intersection property.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:499 advice 18. Overlay polyhedron equals common polyhedron :: ```isabelle lemma geotop_overlay_complex_union: assumes "geotop_polyhedron L1 = geotop_polyhedron L2" shows "geotop_cell_polyhedron (geotop_overlay_complex L1 L2) = geotop_polyhedron L1" ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:505 advice 18. Overlay polyhedron equals common polyhedron :: Proof: - face closure does not add points outside parent overlay cells; - original overlay cells are included in face closure; - original overlay cells cover the common polyhedron.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:512 advice 19. Overlay refines both complexes :: ```isabelle lemma geotop_overlay_complex_refines_left: assumes "A ‚àà geotop_overlay_complex L1 L2" shows "‚àÉœÉ‚ààL1. A ‚äÜ œÉ"
ANSWER_REPORT.md:517 advice 19. Overlay refines both complexes :: lemma geotop_overlay_complex_refines_right: assumes "A ‚àà geotop_overlay_complex L1 L2" shows "‚àÉœÑ‚ààL2. A ‚äÜ œÑ" ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:522 advice 19. Overlay refines both complexes :: For face-closure elements, `A` is a face of some original overlay cell `œÉ‚à©œÑ`, hence `A ‚äÜ œÉ‚à©œÑ ‚äÜ œÉ` and `A ‚äÜ œÑ`.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:524 advice 19. Overlay refines both complexes :: ---
ANSWER_REPORT.md:528 advice Phase 5 ‚Äî Triangulating a cell complex: Hudson Lemma 1.4 :: This is the one nontrivial Hudson lemma worth formalizing.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:532 advice 20. Statement :: ```isabelle theorem geotop_cell_complex_has_simplicial_subdivision: assumes "geotop_cell_complex C" shows "‚àÉM. geotop_simplicial_complex M ‚àß finite M ‚àß geotop_cell_is_subdivision M C" ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:540 advice 20. Statement :: Strictly, `M` is a set of simplices, hence also a cell complex. You may need a coercion lemma:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:542 advice 20. Statement :: ```isabelle lemma simplicial_complex_imp_cell_complex: assumes "geotop_simplicial_complex M" "finite M" shows "geotop_cell_complex M" ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:550 advice 21. Hudson‚Äôs construction :: Hudson‚Äôs Lemma 1.4 says every finite cell complex has a simplicial subdivision with no extra vertices. The proof is inductive over cells ordered by dimension.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:552 advice 21. Hudson‚Äôs construction :: For each cell `A`, choose a distinguished vertex `a(A)` of `A`, typically the first vertex under a global ordering of all vertices.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:554 advice 21. Hudson‚Äôs construction :: Let `B(A)` be the subcomplex of the boundary of `A` consisting of faces not containing `a(A)`.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:556 advice 21. Hudson‚Äôs construction :: Assume all proper faces of `A` have already been triangulated. Define the triangulation of `A` by coning from `a(A)` over the triangulated `B(A)`:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:558 advice 21. Hudson‚Äôs construction :: ```text A' = a(A) * B(A)' ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:562 advice 21. Hudson‚Äôs construction :: This works because:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:564 advice 21. Hudson‚Äôs construction :: ```text A = convex hull {a(A)} ‚à™ |B(A)'| ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:568 advice 21. Hudson‚Äôs construction :: and the triangulation agrees with previously defined triangulations on all faces.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:574 advice Option A: Prove Hudson Lemma 1.4 directly :: You need supporting lemmas:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:576 advice Option A: Prove Hudson Lemma 1.4 directly :: ```isabelle lemma cell_boundary_subcomplex_already_triangulated lemma cone_over_simplicial_complex_is_simplicial_complex lemma cone_over_subdivision_subdivides_cell lemma face_compatibility_of_cell_triangulation ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:583 advice Option A: Prove Hudson Lemma 1.4 directly :: This is conceptually clean, but may take work.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:587 advice Option B: Use barycentric subdivision of the face poset :: For a finite cell complex `C`, define a simplicial complex whose vertices are barycenters of cells of `C`, and whose simplices correspond to chains:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:589 advice Option B: Use barycentric subdivision of the face poset :: ```text A0 < A1 < ... < Ak ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:593 advice Option B: Use barycentric subdivision of the face poset :: where `<` means proper face.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:595 advice Option B: Use barycentric subdivision of the face poset :: Each chain gives simplex:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:597 advice Option B: Use barycentric subdivision of the face poset :: ```text conv {bary(A0), bary(A1), ..., bary(Ak)} ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:601 advice Option B: Use barycentric subdivision of the face poset :: This is the order complex / barycentric subdivision of the cell complex.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:603 advice Option B: Use barycentric subdivision of the face poset :: Pros: - very canonical; - easier to state uniformly; - good compatibility on faces; - close to existing barycentric-subdivision infrastructure.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:609 advice Option B: Use barycentric subdivision of the face poset :: Cons: - need to prove affine independence of barycenters in chains of cells; - need to prove coverage.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:615 advice Recommendation :: If your current development already has strong barycentric subdivision machinery, Option B may be faster. If it is tightly tied to simplices only, Option A may be faster.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:617 advice Recommendation :: For minimal disruption, Option A follows Hudson exactly and avoids general posets.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:621 advice 23. Required output of triangulation theorem :: The triangulation theorem must return `M` satisfying:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:623 advice 23. Required output of triangulation theorem :: ```isabelle geotop_simplicial_complex M finite M ‚ãÉM = geotop_cell_polyhedron C ‚àÄœÅ‚ààM. ‚àÉA‚ààC. œÅ ‚äÜ A ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:630 advice 23. Required output of triangulation theorem :: That is enough. You do not need a stronger isomorphism or stellar-subdivision property.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:632 advice 23. Required output of triangulation theorem :: ---
ANSWER_REPORT.md:636 advice Phase 6 ‚Äî Bridge from cell subdivision to Moise/Munkres subdivision :: Your existing `geotop_is_subdivision` likely expects both inputs to be simplicial complexes and says roughly:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:638 advice Phase 6 ‚Äî Bridge from cell subdivision to Moise/Munkres subdivision :: ```text 1. every simplex of finer complex is contained in a simplex of coarser complex; 2. every simplex of coarser complex is a finite union of finer simplices. ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:643 advice Phase 6 ‚Äî Bridge from cell subdivision to Moise/Munkres subdivision :: The triangulated overlay `M` is simplicial. `L1` and `L2` are simplicial. So prove `M` subdivides each.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:647 advice 24. Left subdivision containment :: ```isabelle lemma triangulated_overlay_simplex_contained_left: assumes "geotop_cell_is_subdivision M (geotop_overlay_complex L1 L2)" assumes "œÅ ‚àà M" shows "‚àÉœÉ‚ààL1. œÅ ‚äÜ œÉ" ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:654 advice 24. Left subdivision containment :: Proof: - `œÅ` lies in some overlay cell `A`; - `A` lies in some `œÉ‚ààL1`.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:660 advice 25. Right subdivision containment :: Analogous.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:664 advice 26. Left polyhedron equality :: ```isabelle lemma triangulated_overlay_polyhedron_left: assumes "geotop_cell_is_subdivision M (geotop_overlay_complex L1 L2)" assumes "geotop_polyhedron L1 = geotop_polyhedron L2" shows "geotop_polyhedron M = geotop_polyhedron L1" ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:671 advice 26. Left polyhedron equality :: Proof: - triangulation covers overlay cell complex; - overlay cell complex covers common polyhedron.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:677 advice 27. Finite union condition :: If `geotop_is_subdivision` includes the Munkres condition that each simplex of the coarse complex is a finite union of finer simplices, derive it from:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:679 advice 27. Finite union condition :: ```text M finite |M| = |L1| every simplex of M is contained in a simplex of L1 ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:685 advice 27. Finite union condition :: For a fixed `œÉ‚ààL1`:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:687 advice 27. Finite union condition :: ```text œÉ = ‚ãÉ {œÅ‚ààM. œÅ ‚äÜ œÉ} ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:691 advice 27. Finite union condition :: Proof: - `‚äÜ`: if `x‚ààœÉ`, since `œÉ‚äÜ|L1|=|M|`, pick `œÅ‚ààM` with `x‚ààœÅ`. By containment, `œÅ‚äÜœÉx` for some `œÉx‚ààL1`. Need show `œÅ‚äÜœÉ`, not merely another simplex. Since `x‚ààœÅ‚à©œÉ`, and `œÅ‚äÜœÉx`, then `x‚ààœÉ‚à©œÉx`. In a simplicial complex, `œÉ‚à©œÉx` is a face of `œÉx` and `œÉ`; but `œÅ` may not be contained in that face automatically unless the refinement respects intersections.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:694 advice 27. Finite union condition :: Better route: - Use the overlay construction directly. If `œÅ‚ààM` and `x‚ààœÅ‚äÜA` for overlay cell `A = œÉ1‚à©œÑ1`, and `x‚ààœÉ`, then `A‚à©œÉ` is a face/subcell of `A` and belongs to the face-closed overlay. Since `M` triangulates the overlay cell complex compatibly with faces, the simplex of `M` containing `x` inside `A` is actually contained in `A‚à©œÉ` when `x` lies in that face.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:697 advice 27. Finite union condition :: This is a technical point. To make the union condition easy, formulate your triangulation theorem as a **subdivision of the cell complex**, not just a cover by simplices. That means it is face-compatible:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:699 advice 27. Finite union condition :: ```text for every cell A‚ààC, the induced subcomplex {œÅ‚ààM | œÅ‚äÜA} covers A. ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:703 advice 27. Finite union condition :: Add this to the triangulation theorem:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:705 advice 27. Finite union condition :: ```isabelle ‚àÄA‚ààC. A = ‚ãÉ {œÅ‚ààM. œÅ ‚äÜ A} ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:709 advice 27. Finite union condition :: Then for `œÉ‚ààL1`, use the fact that `œÉ` is a union of overlay cells contained in `œÉ`:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:711 advice 27. Finite union condition :: ```text œÉ = ‚ãÉ {A‚ààoverlay_complex L1 L2. A ‚äÜ œÉ} ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:715 advice 27. Finite union condition :: and each such `A` is a union of `M`-simplices contained in `A`, hence in `œÉ`.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:717 advice 27. Finite union condition :: So prove:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:719 advice 27. Finite union condition :: ```isabelle lemma overlay_complex_covers_each_left_simplex: assumes "œÉ ‚àà L1" shows "œÉ = ‚ãÉ {A‚ààgeotop_overlay_complex L1 L2. A ‚äÜ œÉ}" ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:725 advice 27. Finite union condition :: Analogously for the right side.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:727 advice 27. Finite union condition :: This avoids a common pitfall.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:731 advice 28. Final bridge lemma :: ```isabelle lemma simplicial_subdivision_from_cell_subdivision_left: assumes "geotop_simplicial_complex L1" assumes "geotop_simplicial_complex M" assumes "finite M" assumes "geotop_cell_is_subdivision M C" assumes "geotop_cell_polyhedron C = geotop_polyhedron L1" assumes "‚àÄA‚ààC. ‚àÉœÉ‚ààL1. A ‚äÜ œÉ" assumes "‚àÄœÉ‚ààL1. œÉ = ‚ãÉ {A‚ààC. A ‚äÜ œÉ}" shows "geotop_is_subdivision M L1" ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:743 advice 28. Final bridge lemma :: Use this for both `L1` and `L2`.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:745 advice 28. Final bridge lemma :: ---
ANSWER_REPORT.md:751 advice 29. Main theorem :: ```isabelle theorem geotop_common_subdivision_finite: fixes L1 L2 :: "'a::euclidean_space set set" assumes hL1: "geotop_simplicial_complex L1" assumes hL2: "geotop_simplicial_complex L2" assumes fL1: "finite L1" assumes fL2: "finite L2" assumes hpoly: "geotop_polyhedron L1 = geotop_polyhedron L2" shows "‚àÉM. geotop_is_subdivision M L1 ‚àß geotop_is_subdivision M L2" proof - let ?C = "geotop_overlay_complex L1 L2" have Ccx: "geotop_cell_complex ?C" using hL1 hL2 fL1 fL2 by (rule geotop_overlay_complex_is_cell_complex)
ANSWER_REPORT.md:766 advice 29. Main theorem :: obtain M where Msimp: "geotop_simplicial_complex M" and Mfin: "finite M" and MsubC: "geotop_cell_is_subdivision M ?C" and Minduced: "‚àÄA‚àà?C. A = ‚ãÉ {œÅ‚ààM. œÅ ‚äÜ A}" using geotop_cell_complex_has_simplicial_subdivision[OF Ccx] by blast
ANSWER_REPORT.md:774 advice 29. Main theorem :: have MsubL1: "geotop_is_subdivision M L1" using hL1 Msimp Mfin MsubC hpoly geotop_overlay_complex_union geotop_overlay_complex_refines_left geotop_overlay_complex_covers_each_left_simplex Minduced by (rule simplicial_subdivision_from_cell_subdivision_left)
ANSWER_REPORT.md:782 advice 29. Main theorem :: have MsubL2: "geotop_is_subdivision M L2" using hL2 Msimp Mfin MsubC hpoly geotop_overlay_complex_union geotop_overlay_complex_refines_right geotop_overlay_complex_covers_each_right_simplex Minduced by (rule simplicial_subdivision_from_cell_subdivision_right)
ANSWER_REPORT.md:790 advice 29. Main theorem :: show ?thesis using MsubL1 MsubL2 by blast qed ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:794 advice 29. Main theorem :: This is the theorem that should replace the old `Sd^N` route.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:796 advice 29. Main theorem :: ---
ANSWER_REPORT.md:800 advice Phase 8 ‚Äî Reprove Moise Theorem 1 :: Your original theorem:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:802 advice Phase 8 ‚Äî Reprove Moise Theorem 1 :: ```isabelle theorem Theorem_GT_1: fixes K L1 L2 :: "'a::euclidean_space set set" assumes hKfin: "finite K" assumes hL1: "geotop_is_subdivision L1 K" assumes hL2: "geotop_is_subdivision L2 K" shows "‚àÉL. geotop_is_subdivision L L1 ‚àß geotop_is_subdivision L L2" ```

ANSWER_REPORT.md:831 advice Phase 8 ‚Äî Reprove Moise Theorem 1 :: No barycentric subdivision is needed.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:833 advice Phase 8 ‚Äî Reprove Moise Theorem 1 :: ---
ANSWER_REPORT.md:839 advice Check 1 ‚Äî This does not assert the false statement :: The new construction does **not** claim that some `Sd^N K` aligns with the subdivision boundaries. It creates new vertices along the overlay boundaries. Therefore it avoids the dyadic-versus-`1/3` counterexample.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:843 advice Check 2 ‚Äî Literal containment is preserved :: Every final simplex lies inside an overlay cell. Every overlay cell lies inside one simplex of `L1` and one simplex of `L2`. Therefore literal containment is immediate.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:845 advice Check 2 ‚Äî Literal containment is preserved :: This is exactly what the PL inverse-image transport needs.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:849 advice Check 3 ‚Äî Finiteness is preserved :: If `L1` and `L2` are finite, then:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:851 advice Check 3 ‚Äî Finiteness is preserved :: ```text number of raw overlay cells ‚â§ |L1| ¬∑ |L2| ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:855 advice Check 3 ‚Äî Finiteness is preserved :: Taking all faces is still finite because each cell has finitely many faces. Hudson‚Äôs triangulation of a finite cell complex is finite.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:859 advice Check 4 ‚Äî The union condition is not automatic from containment :: Be careful: proving every fine simplex is contained in a coarse simplex plus equality of total polyhedra is not always enough to show each coarse simplex is a union of fine simplices unless the fine triangulation is compatible with the coarse simplex boundaries.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:861 advice Check 4 ‚Äî The union condition is not automatic from containment :: This is why the overlay complex must be face-closed and the triangulation theorem must give induced subdivisions on every cell.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:865 advice Check 5 ‚Äî The theorem works for arbitrary subdivisions :: The input complexes need not be related by barycentric subdivision, stellar subdivision, or derived subdivision. Overlay construction works for arbitrary finite Euclidean simplicial complexes with equal polyhedron.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:867 advice Check 5 ‚Äî The theorem works for arbitrary subdivisions :: ---
ANSWER_REPORT.md:873 advice If your existing `geotop_is_subdivision` only requires containment plus polyhedron equality :: Then Phases 6.27‚Äì6.28 simplify greatly. But the report says you use Munkres‚Äô two-part definition, so assume the finite-union condition is present.

ANSWER_REPORT.md:879 advice If Isabelle already has `polytope` and `polyhedron` lemmas :: ```text simplex is polytope polytope intersection polytope polytope has finitely many faces polytope admits triangulation ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:886 advice If Isabelle already has `polytope` and `polyhedron` lemmas :: This could substantially shorten Phases 1‚Äì5.

Add definitions for `geotop_cell`, `geotop_cell_face`, `geotop_cell_complex`, `geotop_cell_polyhedron`, `geotop_cell_is_subdivision`. 2. Prove simplex/cell bridge lemmas. 3. Prove cell-face finite/closure lemmas. 4. Define raw overlay cells and face-closed overlay complex. 5. Prove overlay complex is a finite cell complex. 6. Prove overlay refines both `L1` and `L2`. 7. Replace the proof of `Theorem_GT_1` with the new theorem. 11. Remove or quarantine the false `geotop_iterated_Sd_refines_subdivision` t...
ANSWER_REPORT.md:908 advice Suggested order of implementation :: ---
ANSWER_REPORT.md:914 advice Hard point A ‚Äî finite faces of cells :: If using a halfspace representation, finite faces are finite subsets of active inequalities. This is straightforward but needs setup.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:916 advice Hard point A ‚Äî finite faces of cells :: If using convex hull representation, you may need a theorem that a polytope has finitely many faces.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:920 advice Hard point B ‚Äî overlay intersection property :: This is the main geometric verification. Face closure helps, but you still need to show intersections of cells in the face-closed overlay are common faces.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:922 advice Hard point B ‚Äî overlay intersection property :: If it becomes painful, switch to a hyperplane-arrangement representation of the overlay cells. All cells are then sign/equality-pattern regions, and common-face behavior becomes syntactic.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:926 advice Hard point C ‚Äî compatible triangulation :: A triangulation of each cell separately is not enough. It must agree on shared faces. Hudson Lemma 1.4 does this by induction over the cell complex. The order-complex/barycentric triangulation also does this canonically.
ANSWER_REPORT.md:928 advice Hard point C ‚Äî compatible triangulation :: ---
ANSWER_REPORT.md:932 advice Final recommendation :: The most robust route is:
ANSWER_REPORT.md:934 advice Final recommendation :: ```text raw intersections œÉ‚à©œÑ ‚Üí close under faces ‚Üí finite convex cell complex C ‚Üí canonical barycentric/order-complex triangulation Sd(C) ‚Üí common subdivision M ```
ANSWER_REPORT.md:942 advice Final recommendation :: This avoids the false iterated-barycentric-refinement theorem and gives the exact literal containment needed for Moise Theorem 1 and the downstream PL-transport construction.
CLAUDE.md:4 advice First pass: a "faithful formal proof sketch pass": for each stated :: theorem corresponding to the book, create a compiling proof sketch (i.e. with sorries) that will faithfully mirror the geotop.tex proof.
CLAUDE.md:8 advice Note: while it's OK to initially just write the proof in comments (if it's easier), :: this pass really has to ultimately produce a nontrivial formal proof sketch. Just a comment is not a "faithful formal proof sketch".
CLAUDE.md:15 advice ‚ùó ABSOLUTE RULE: New Proof Workflow :: When writing **any new proof code** (skeleton, step, or edit):
CLAUDE.md:17 advice ‚ùó ABSOLUTE RULE: New Proof Workflow :: 1. **Only use `sorry`.** 2. **No exceptions** ‚Äî do not write:
CLAUDE.md:20 advice ‚ùó ABSOLUTE RULE: New Proof Workflow :: * `by ...`, `done` * `simp`, `auto`, `blast`, `metis`, `linarith` * `rule`, `erule`, `drule`, etc. 3. Write the full proof structure using `sorry`, then **compile immediately**. 4. Only after a successful build:
CLAUDE.md:26 advice ‚ùó ABSOLUTE RULE: New Proof Workflow :: * replace `sorry`s in **small batches (3‚Äì5)** using `sledgehammer` / `process_theories` 5. If sledgehammer fails, you can very cautiously experiment with a subproof. 6. Instead of `by blast` etc always use our safe `by (by100 blast)` , `by (by100 fast)` , `by (by100 simp)`, etc methods that limit the time to 100 ms, or the restricted apply (rule ...), apply (erule ...), apply (drule ...) ). 7. If you catch yourself writing anything other than `sorry`: ‚Üí **STOP and replace it with `sorry`**

CLAUDE_ISA_TOP.md:4 advice Rules for  Formalizing /project/geotop.tex statements (no proofs) in Isabelle/HOL :: - You should assume that everything in Isabelle Complex_Main is available This includes a lot of math already - don't repeat those!
CLAUDE_ISA_TOP.md:9 advice No Duplication and Extra Content :: - There may be no duplicates of the statements from Complex_Main - There may be no lemmas/theorems that do not have a counterpart in geotop.tex . Everything has to be clearly marked with the geotop.tex counterpart.
CLAUDE_ISA_TOP.md:13 advice Work Strategy :: - Your task is to formalize **all** definitions and statements in geotop.tex, **no proofs** yet (everything admitted but no axioms). - Everything from geotop.tex has to have before it a clear comment about its origin in geotop.tex like this (you must also add the line number in geotop.tex): (** from ¬ß48 Lemma 48.1 (direction): open-set Baire implies closed-set Baire **) (** LATEX VERSION: Lemma 48.1: X is Baire iff countable intersections of dense open sets are dense. **) - Do this in the geotop.tex order, page by page, chapter by chapter, patiently, in smaller batches. It can take the whole day. Quality is critical, speed is not. - You can create extra definitions as needed/suitable for ...
CLAUDE_ISA_TOP.md:38 advice Logic System :: - **This is classical Isabelle/HOL** (not constructive) - everybody know that, right?
CLAUDE_ISA_TOP.md:46 advice ‚úÖ DEFINITIONS must be SUBSTANTIVE :: - Definitions must capture actual mathematical content - **NEVER create stub definitions that just return `True`** - If you cannot make a definition substantive, DO NOT add it - instead, start gradually defining the needed concepts so that you can add the definition after some work
CLAUDE_ISA_TOP.md:54 advice Good vs Bad Definitions :: When they are NOT acceptable:
CLAUDE_ISA_TOP.md:56 advice Good vs Bad Definitions :: - Predicates that just return True without capturing the concept - Definitions that provide no mathematical content - Any definition where True appears as the main body - simplified or a stub
CLAUDE_ISA_TOP.md:61 advice Good vs Bad Definitions :: "No proofs yet" applies to THEOREMS, not DEFINITIONS:
CLAUDE_ISA_TOP.md:63 advice Good vs Bad Definitions :: - ‚úÖ Theorems: State correctly, use sorry. for proof - ‚ùå Definitions: Must be substantive and perfect, no simplifications. - The distinction: Theorems make claims (no proof). Definitions establish meaning (must be meaningful).

DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:3 advice `.dev34_fast_cache` and `check_dev34_fast.sh`: Report and Manual :: Date: 2026-06-10
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:5 advice `.dev34_fast_cache` and `check_dev34_fast.sh`: Report and Manual :: This report explains the fast-checking approach currently used for the GeoTop Sections 3-4 work. It covers the local cache directory `.dev34_fast_cache`, the driver script `check_dev34_fast.sh`, the many available modes, where the speedup comes from, what is and is not certified by each command, and the downsides that matter for closing the remaining Moise Theorems 3.3, 3.4, 3.7, 4.2, and 4.4 packages.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:12 advice `.dev34_fast_cache` and `check_dev34_fast.sh`: Report and Manual :: The short answer is: yes, it is faster for focused proof iteration. The main speedup comes from reusing finished Isabelle heaps and from building a generated prefix of a long theory once, then processing only the active theorem slice. It is not a replacement for final verification. It is a way to make the edit-check loop short enough that we can keep proving the remaining hard geometric steps without spending minutes on unchanged context every time.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:19 advice `.dev34_fast_cache` and `check_dev34_fast.sh`: Report and Manual :: As of this review, the local cache directory under `tst/.dev34_fast_cache` is about 72 MB. It contains 153 generated `split-*` directories, 339 top-level SHA-256 stamp files, and 5 process-cache stamp files. Those numbers are descriptive, not goals. They show the normal scale of the current development cache. The actual Isabelle heap images are managed by Isabelle because the script builds with `system_heaps=true`; `.dev34_fast_cache` mostly stores freshness stamps and generated split theories.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:29 advice 1. Why the Fast Cache Exists :: The GeoTop Sections 3-4 proof stack is split into many local development sessions:
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:32 advice 1. Why the Fast Cache Exists :: ```text dev34_pre dev34_prefix_base dev34_prefix_graph/cache dev34_prefix_graph dev34_prefix_mid dev34_prefix dev34_facts dev34_workfacts dev34_linkfacts dev34_graphfacts dev34_graphwork dev34_openstar dev34_core dev34 ```
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:49 advice 1. Why the Fast Cache Exists :: This layering is useful because it lets us avoid rebuilding all of `GeoTop` after every small edit. Without the fast helper, a verification attempt near the end of `dev34_prefix_mid/GeoTop_3_4_Prefix_Mid.thy` may replay thousands of lines that have not changed. That cost is tolerable at milestones but too expensive for the normal proof loop, especially when the current proof is still being developed with `sorry`-first skeletons, sledgehammer probes, and bounded tactic replacements.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:57 advice 1. Why the Fast Cache Exists :: The script addresses two different sources of repeated work.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:59 advice 1. Why the Fast Cache Exists :: First, it chooses the smallest useful parent heap. When editing a file in `dev34_prefix_mid`, the parent logic is `GeoTop34PrefixGraphDirty`, not the entire final `GeoTop34Dev` stack. If the `prefix-graph` heap is fresh, Isabelle can load that heap and process the mid-prefix file from there.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:64 advice 1. Why the Fast Cache Exists :: Second, it can split a long theory at a named theorem or lemma. The generated prefix before the target is built once. The active theorem slice is then processed against that generated prefix session. For example, the named focus target `mid-split-free` splits `dev34_prefix_mid/GeoTop_3_4_Prefix_Mid.thy` at `theorem Theorem_GT_3_3`. When the edit is inside that theorem package, the already-finished prefix above it does not need to be replayed on every run.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:72 advice 1. Why the Fast Cache Exists :: This is the core idea: cache stable context, then recheck only the part that changed.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:77 advice 2. The Cache Directory :: The cache directory is local to `tst/`:
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:79 advice 2. The Cache Directory :: ```text tst/.dev34_fast_cache/ ```
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:83 advice 2. The Cache Directory :: It is disposable. It is not a proof artifact and should not be treated as part of the mathematical result. If removed, the source theories remain the source of truth; the script will rebuild the needed cache entries as work continues.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:87 advice 2. The Cache Directory :: There are four main kinds of cache entries.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:91 advice 2.1 Layer stamps :: Layer stamps are small files such as:
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:93 advice 2.1 Layer stamps :: ```text .dev34_fast_cache/pre.sha256 .dev34_fast_cache/prefix-base.sha256 .dev34_fast_cache/prefix-graph-cache.sha256 .dev34_fast_cache/prefix-graph.sha256 .dev34_fast_cache/prefix-mid.sha256 .dev34_fast_cache/prefix.sha256 .dev34_fast_cache/core.sha256 .dev34_fast_cache/dev34.sha256 ```
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:104 advice 2.1 Layer stamps :: Each stamp records a SHA-256 digest computed from the target name, the Isabelle session name, the proof-forcing setting, and the source files that feed that layer. The stamp does not contain the heap. It says that the corresponding Isabelle heap was successfully built for the exact inputs represented by the digest.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:110 advice 2.1 Layer stamps :: Freshness is content-based. If an input theory or `ROOT` file changes, the digest changes and the layer is stale. If the digest still matches, the script can skip rebuilding that layer.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:116 advice 2.2 Process stamps :: Process stamps have names like:
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:118 advice 2.2 Process stamps :: ```text .dev34_fast_cache/proc-<hash>.sha256 ```
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:122 advice 2.2 Process stamps :: They record that a file-level `process_theories` check succeeded against a particular parent logic and parent digest. These stamps are useful when running the same `hot` command twice without source changes. The second run can be skipped because the previous process result is still fresh.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:127 advice 2.2 Process stamps :: The important practical point is that process stamps are proof-loop accelerators, not timing measurements. If the question is "how long does this proof slice actually take now?", disable process-cache skipping:
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:131 advice 2.2 Process stamps :: ```bash DEV34_FAST_PROC_CACHE=0 ./check_dev34_fast.sh focus mid-split-free ```
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:137 advice 2.3 Split directories :: Split directories are generated sessions:
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:139 advice 2.3 Split directories :: ```text .dev34_fast_cache/split-<12hex>/ ```
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:143 advice 2.3 Split directories :: A typical split directory contains:
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:145 advice 2.3 Split directories :: ```text ROOT GeoTop_3_4_Prefix_Mid_Split_<key>_Prefix.thy GeoTop_3_4_Prefix_Mid_Split_<key>_Slice.thy ```
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:151 advice 2.3 Split directories :: The prefix theory copies the original theory up to the split point and imports the real parent logic or a previous chained generated prefix. The slice theory imports that prefix session and copies only the active top-level theorem or lemma range. A full-tail check creates a `_Tail.thy` instead of checking only one top-level slice.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:157 advice 2.3 Split directories :: The generated files explain why error locations sometimes point into `.dev34_fast_cache/split-*`. The text is copied from the real source, so the fix still belongs in the original theory file. The printed line interval from the script is the guide for mapping the generated error back to the source.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:164 advice 2.4 Split stamps :: Split stamps have names like:
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:166 advice 2.4 Split stamps :: ```text .dev34_fast_cache/split-<longhash>.sha256 ```
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:170 advice 2.4 Split stamps :: These record freshness for generated split prefixes and split slice/tail process runs. The digest includes the original file, split pattern, generated `ROOT`, generated prefix or slice file, parent digest, chained prefix digest when applicable, and proof-mode flags.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:175 advice 2.4 Split stamps :: This means an edit before the split point invalidates the generated prefix, while an edit inside the target theorem usually invalidates only the slice. That distinction is the main reason the current loop is faster after the first warm run.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:182 advice 3. Parent Heaps and Layer Mapping :: The script has two closely related mappings.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:184 advice 3. Parent Heaps and Layer Mapping :: `parent_context` maps a file to the Isabelle logic and `-d` directories used by `process_theories`. For example:
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:187 advice 3. Parent Heaps and Layer Mapping :: ```text dev34_prefix_mid/* -> parent logic GeoTop34PrefixGraphDirty dev34_prefix/* -> parent logic GeoTop34PrefixMidDirty dev34_core/* -> parent logic GeoTop34OpenStarDirty dev34/* -> parent logic GeoTop34CoreDirty ```
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:194 advice 3. Parent Heaps and Layer Mapping :: `parent_target_for_file` maps a file to the cache layer that should be fresh before processing that file:
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:197 advice 3. Parent Heaps and Layer Mapping :: ```text dev34_prefix_mid/* -> prefix-graph dev34_prefix/* -> prefix-mid dev34_core/* -> openstar dev34/* -> core ```
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:204 advice 3. Parent Heaps and Layer Mapping :: This is why a `focus mid-split-free` run can say that `prefix-graph` is fresh and then jump directly to the generated prefix/slice work for the mid-prefix file. It is not rebuilding the full dev34 stack.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:208 advice 3. Parent Heaps and Layer Mapping :: The script can also warm the whole parent chain. By default `DEV34_FAST_DEEP_AUTOWARM=1`, so if a parent is stale, the script walks through the earlier layers and rebuilds the missing or stale pieces. That first cold run can still be slow, but it is buying future fast runs.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:215 advice 4. The Commands Worth Knowing :: The script exposes many modes because the development loop has several different tasks: search, hole inventory, parent warming, file processing, split processing, named target focus, and milestone builds. In practice, a small subset handles most work.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:222 advice 4.1 Search and inventory :: Use these frequently:
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:224 advice 4.1 Search and inventory :: ```bash ./check_dev34_fast.sh holes ./check_dev34_fast.sh scan "selected boundary edge" ./check_dev34_fast.sh index "polygon disk chord subdisk" ./check_dev34_fast.sh names "free_triangle" ./check_dev34_fast.sh stmts "finite linear graph" ```
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:232 advice 4.1 Search and inventory :: `holes` greps the active dev34 target theories for `sorry` and `sledgehammer`. It is a status map, not a build.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:235 advice 4.1 Search and inventory :: `scan` greps the generated indexes and the active target theories. This is the command that answers the user's point about searching the full index. It should be used before adding helpers and again when a proof direction stalls. It is cheap, and it prevents wasted work.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:240 advice 4.1 Search and inventory :: `index`, `names`, and `stmts` narrow the search to index files. They are useful when the pattern is noisy in source files.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:245 advice 4.2 Planning and cache status :: Use these to understand what will be checked:
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:247 advice 4.2 Planning and cache status :: ```bash ./check_dev34_fast.sh dirty ./check_dev34_fast.sh plan dev34_prefix_mid/GeoTop_3_4_Prefix_Mid.thy ./check_dev34_fast.sh cache-status dev34_prefix_mid/GeoTop_3_4_Prefix_Mid.thy ./check_dev34_fast.sh focus-status mid-split-free ```
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:254 advice 4.2 Planning and cache status :: `plan` shows the parent logic and whether the reusable parent cache is fresh. `focus-status` checks the named split target without building. It tells whether the parent, split prefix, and slice process stamps are fresh.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:260 advice 4.3 Warming :: Use these when switching targets or before a longer proof session:
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:262 advice 4.3 Warming :: ```bash ./check_dev34_fast.sh warm dev34_prefix_mid/GeoTop_3_4_Prefix_Mid.thy ./check_dev34_fast.sh focus-warm mid-split-free ./check_dev34_fast.sh focus-prime mid-split-free mid-d42 ./check_dev34_fast.sh cache-parents dev34_prefix_mid/GeoTop_3_4_Prefix_Mid.thy ```
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:269 advice 4.3 Warming :: `warm` prepares the parent heap for a file. `focus-warm` prepares the generated prefix before a named focus target. `focus-prime` does that for one or more named targets. The first warm pass can be expensive, but the next target check is usually much faster.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:276 advice 4.4 Normal proof checks :: These are the main iteration commands:
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:278 advice 4.4 Normal proof checks :: ```bash ./check_dev34_fast.sh hot dev34_prefix_mid/GeoTop_3_4_Prefix_Mid.thy ./check_dev34_fast.sh slice-hot dev34_prefix_mid/GeoTop_3_4_Prefix_Mid.thy "theorem Theorem_GT_3_3" ./check_dev34_fast.sh focus mid-split-free ```
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:284 advice 4.4 Normal proof checks :: `hot` processes the whole file against its parent heap. It is narrower than a layer build but still replays the whole file.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:287 advice 4.4 Normal proof checks :: `slice-hot` builds or reuses a generated prefix and processes only the next top-level theorem/lemma slice beginning at the first pattern match. It is the best loop when we are editing one hard theorem in a long file.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:291 advice 4.4 Normal proof checks :: `focus` is a named wrapper around `slice-hot` plus a full-index scan. The named focus targets currently include:
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:294 advice 4.4 Normal proof checks :: ```text graph-branch graph-branch-local graph-cycle-cut mid-split-free mid-fold mid-support mid-d42 prefix-d44 dev34-cycle dev34-cycle-realization dev34-fan dev34-semicircle ```
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:309 advice 4.4 Normal proof checks :: For the current Moise 3.3 work, the normal command is:
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:311 advice 4.4 Normal proof checks :: ```bash TIMEOUT=240s ./check_dev34_fast.sh focus mid-split-free ```
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:317 advice 4.5 Scaffold checks :: Use these only for `sorry`-first structure checks:
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:319 advice 4.5 Scaffold checks :: ```bash ./check_dev34_fast.sh outline dev34_prefix_mid/GeoTop_3_4_Prefix_Mid.thy ./check_dev34_fast.sh focus-outline mid-split-free ```
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:324 advice 4.5 Scaffold checks :: These paths set `SKIP_PROOFS=1`. They are useful after adding a statement skeleton with `sorry`, but they do not validate proof replacements.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:329 advice 4.6 Broader checks :: Use these at package boundaries:
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:331 advice 4.6 Broader checks :: ```bash ./check_dev34_fast.sh cache-through dev34_prefix_mid/GeoTop_3_4_Prefix_Mid.thy ./check_dev34_fast.sh auto dev34_prefix_mid/GeoTop_3_4_Prefix_Mid.thy FORCE_PROOFS=1 ./check_dev34_fast.sh prove dev34_prefix_mid/GeoTop_3_4_Prefix_Mid.thy ```
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:337 advice 4.6 Broader checks :: `cache-through` builds all cache layers through the target layer, skipping fresh layers. `auto` builds the smallest dirty or explicit layer. `prove` forces proof checking options through the wrapper. These are slower than `focus`, but they provide a wider confidence boundary.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:342 advice 4.6 Broader checks :: The final end condition is still broader than the fast helper: all target `sorry`s closed, indexes regenerated, and a real Isabelle build consistent with the project instructions.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:348 advice 5. Environment Options :: Important environment variables:
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:350 advice 5. Environment Options :: ```text TIMEOUT=240s Override command timeout. Useful for current hard targets.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:354 advice 5. Environment Options :: FORCE_PROOFS=1 Pass proof-forcing options into the wrapper and include the setting in cache digests. Use for milestone checks, not every tiny edit.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:358 advice 5. Environment Options :: SKIP_PROOFS=1 Used by outline modes. Good for skeleton checking only.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:361 advice 5. Environment Options :: DEV34_FAST_PROC_CACHE=0 Disable skipping of previously successful process runs. Use when measuring actual runtime or when a truly fresh process check is wanted.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:365 advice 5. Environment Options :: DEV34_FAST_AUTOWARM=0 Do not automatically warm parent heaps before hot or split checks.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:368 advice 5. Environment Options :: DEV34_FAST_DEEP_AUTOWARM=0 Warm only the immediate parent target instead of the whole parent chain.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:371 advice 5. Environment Options :: DEV34_FAST_SKIP_FRESH_TARGET=0 Process a file even if the whole target layer cache is already fresh.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:374 advice 5. Environment Options :: FORCE_CACHE=1 Rebuild a layer cache even when its stamp matches.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:377 advice 5. Environment Options :: DEV34_FAST_VERBOSE=1 Print generated split-session details. ```
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:381 advice 5. Environment Options :: The default settings favor fast theorem iteration. The stricter settings are for milestone validation, debugging the wrapper, or measuring runtime.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:386 advice 6. Is It Faster? :: For the current workflow, yes. The gain is largest when all of these are true:
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:388 advice 6. Is It Faster? :: 1. The target theorem is late in a large file. 2. The edit is inside the target theorem or a nearby local helper. 3. The parent heap is fresh. 4. The generated split prefix is fresh. 5. The active theorem slice does not contain a tactic that itself takes minutes.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:395 advice 6. Is It Faster? :: The expected timing pattern is:
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:397 advice 6. Is It Faster? :: ```text Cold parent build: slow; prepares reusable heap state. Cold split-prefix build: still slow; prepares the prefix before the target. Warm slice check: much faster; checks only the active theorem slice. Fresh process-cache rerun: near-instant; skips an identical successful run. ```
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:404 advice 6. Is It Faster? :: This explains why a first run after a target switch may still take a long time. If output says `cache build`, `cache warm-through`, or `building prefix cache`, the script is doing setup work. The meaningful comparison is the next run after the prefix and parent are fresh.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:409 advice 6. Is It Faster? :: If output says `skipped prefix cache` and then spends a long time on `processing slice`, the cache is not the bottleneck. The bottleneck is inside the active proof text. At that point the right response is to split the proof more finely, replace broad automation with bounded methods such as `by (by100 blast)` or explicit rule steps, and search the full index for sharper facts.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:418 advice 7. Downsides and Risks :: The main downside is that there are many modes. That is manageable if we use a small default set:
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:421 advice 7. Downsides and Risks :: ```bash ./check_dev34_fast.sh scan PATTERN ./check_dev34_fast.sh holes ./check_dev34_fast.sh focus TARGET ./check_dev34_fast.sh focus-status TARGET ./check_dev34_fast.sh cache-through FILE ```
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:429 advice 7. Downsides and Risks :: The second downside is cache invalidation. Edits above a split point invalidate the generated prefix. This is correct, but it means a large statement move or early-helper edit can make the next run expensive again.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:433 advice 7. Downsides and Risks :: The third downside is local clutter. The cache accumulates split directories for old targets and old patterns. `./check_dev34_fast.sh cache-clean` removes the directory, but that also discards useful warm state. Normal theorem iteration should not clean it unless the cache behavior is confusing or the script itself is being debugged.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:439 advice 7. Downsides and Risks :: The fourth downside is narrow confidence. A successful `focus mid-split-free` means the generated slice for the named target processed against its generated prefix. It does not mean later theories or other sections still build. That is why package-level progress should be followed by `cache-through`, hole scans, index regeneration, and eventually a broader build.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:445 advice 7. Downsides and Risks :: The fifth downside is pattern sensitivity. `slice-hot FILE PATTERN` uses the first `rg` match. If the pattern is too broad and matches a comment or earlier helper, the split point is wrong. Named `focus` targets reduce this risk by encoding exact patterns for the known hard proof packages.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:450 advice 7. Downsides and Risks :: The sixth downside is script maintenance. The script hashes the files and session structure it knows about. If we add new session files, move theory files, or change index generation conventions, `check_dev34_fast.sh`, `gen_index.sh`, and `gen_stmt_index.sh` need to be reviewed together. This matches the current instruction to refresh the index scripts when session files change.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:459 advice 8. Correctness Boundaries :: The cache is conservative about local freshness, but it is not a proof system. The source theories and Isabelle are the proof system.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:462 advice 8. Correctness Boundaries :: The commands form a confidence ladder:
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:464 advice 8. Correctness Boundaries :: ```text holes / scan / plan / focus-status Inspection only. No proof validation.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:468 advice 8. Correctness Boundaries :: outline / focus-outline Statement and skeleton validation with skip_proofs=true.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:471 advice 8. Correctness Boundaries :: hot / slice-hot / focus Main proof-loop validation for a file or generated theorem slice.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:474 advice 8. Correctness Boundaries :: cache-through / auto Wider layer validation through a target layer.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:477 advice 8. Correctness Boundaries :: FORCE_PROOFS=1 prove Stricter wrapper-level milestone check.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:480 advice 8. Correctness Boundaries :: Full project build Final confidence boundary for the source tree. ```
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:484 advice 8. Correctness Boundaries :: The important operational rule is to match the command to the claim. A slice check is enough to say "this edit compiles in the active slice." It is not enough to say "Moise Section 3 is closed." A theorem package closure needs a broader check and the live hole map must go down.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:491 advice 9. Recommended Workflow for the Remaining Moise Work :: For the active Theorem 3.3 split/free package:
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:493 advice 9. Recommended Workflow for the Remaining Moise Work :: ```bash ./check_dev34_fast.sh scan "subdisk free triangle carrier count" TIMEOUT=240s ./check_dev34_fast.sh focus-status mid-split-free TIMEOUT=240s ./check_dev34_fast.sh focus mid-split-free ```
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:499 advice 9. Recommended Workflow for the Remaining Moise Work :: Before adding a helper, search the full index. If an exact lemma exists, use it. If no exact lemma exists, add only the next faithful Moise bridge, first with `sorry`, then compile immediately. After that, replace small groups of `sorry`s with bounded proof methods or sledgehammer suggestions.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:504 advice 9. Recommended Workflow for the Remaining Moise Work :: After a meaningful change:
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:506 advice 9. Recommended Workflow for the Remaining Moise Work :: ```bash bash gen_index.sh && bash gen_stmt_index.sh ./check_dev34_fast.sh holes git commit ```
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:512 advice 9. Recommended Workflow for the Remaining Moise Work :: The commit message should carry the plan and the outcome: which Moise theorem was targeted, which bridge was attempted, what checked, what remained open, and whether a previous strategy had to be corrected. This is better than continually appending to progress reports because the history remains tied to the source changes.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:518 advice 9. Recommended Workflow for the Remaining Moise Work :: When a named package closes:
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:520 advice 9. Recommended Workflow for the Remaining Moise Work :: ```bash TIMEOUT=240s ./check_dev34_fast.sh cache-through dev34_prefix_mid/GeoTop_3_4_Prefix_Mid.thy ./check_dev34_fast.sh holes bash gen_index.sh && bash gen_stmt_index.sh ```
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:526 advice 9. Recommended Workflow for the Remaining Moise Work :: Then move to the next live hole using the same pattern.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:530 advice 10. Bottom Line :: The `.dev34_fast_cache` approach is a local development accelerator. It gives fast feedback by separating stable parent context, stable in-file prefixes, and the active theorem slice. It is faster when used on a named target with a warm parent and a warm split prefix. It has downsides: extra modes, local clutter, pattern sensitivity, narrow confidence, and maintenance requirements when the session layout changes.
DEV34_FAST_CACHE_APPROACH_REPORT_2026_06_10.md:537 advice 10. Bottom Line :: The right use is not to trust it blindly. The right use is to run it constantly for focused proof work, search the full index frequently, broaden checks at package boundaries, regenerate the indexes after source changes, and reserve full builds for milestone certification. Used that way, it directly addresses the 2-3 minute iteration problem without changing the proof obligations we still have to close.

The low-level theorem should be changed to that collar form before proof effort is spent there.

PLAN1.md:425 advice Correct classical argument for E (documented for future work) :: **Proof sketch** (from Moise early.tex): 1. open_star K v = ‚ãÉ{rel_int œÑ : œÑ ‚àà K, v ‚àà œÑ}, a finite union in a finite complex K' = K. 2. For x, y ‚àà T in rel_int œÉ_1, rel_int œÉ_2 (possibly equal), the segment xy must stay in open_star K v (T convex). 3. If œÉ_1 ‚âÝ œÉ_2: at generic interior point of xy, z = (1-t)x + ty is NOT in affine hull œÉ_1 ‚à™ affine hull œÉ_2 (when œÉ_1 ‚äÑ œÉ_2 and vv). Hence z ‚àâ œÉ_1 ‚à™ œÉ_2. z must be in some œÉ_3 ‚àã v with different affine hull. But iterating this around the segment gives infinitely many simplices for a finite complex ‚Äî contradiction. 4. So all points of T are in one œÉ (call it œÉ_0 = the unique simplex with x ‚àà rel_int œÉ_0 for some x ‚àà T). Then T ‚äÜ œÉ_0.
PLAN1.md:438 advice Correct classical argument for E (documented for future work) :: Formalising step 3 in Isabelle requires: (a) `affine_independent_span_eq_aff_hull` equivalents, (b) finite complex ‚üπ finite relevant œÉ's bound, (c) the "segment leaves finite union" classical lemma. ~100 additional lines.

PLAN1.md:467 advice Subtle issue discovered: Moise's proof needs more than star cover :: The naive Lebesgue-via-vertex-stars argument gives T ‚äÜ closed_star(v) = ‚ãÉ{œÉ ‚àà K' : v ‚àà œÉ}, NOT T ‚äÜ single œÉ ‚àã v. Example: K' = {[0, 0.5], [0.5, 1], {0}, {0.5}, {1}}, v = {0.5}, T = [0.3, 0.7] is convex, T ‚äÜ open_star(v) = (0, 1), but T is not in any single simplex of K'.
PLAN1.md:472 advice Subtle issue discovered: Moise's proof needs more than star cover :: So the claim "T convex ‚äÜ open_star K' v ‚üπ T ‚äÜ single œÉ" is FALSE.
PLAN1.md:474 advice Subtle issue discovered: Moise's proof needs more than star cover :: **Correct classical argument**: use a STRONGER Lebesgue-like lemma (early.tex Lemma 4.12):
PLAN1.md:477 advice Subtle issue discovered: Moise's proof needs more than star cover :: > ‚àÉŒ¥ > 0. ‚àÄT ‚äÜ |K'|. diam T < Œ¥ ‚üπ ‚àÉœÉ ‚àà K'. T ‚äÜ œÉ.
PLAN1.md:479 advice Subtle issue discovered: Moise's proof needs more than star cover :: Proof uses the "barycentric star" cover (not just vertex star), where each barycentric star is guaranteed to fit in a single simplex. Specifically, the barycentric subdivision Sd(K') has vertex stars that are (subsets of) unique simplices of K'. This links to D: the barycentric subdivision construction is needed for a clean E proof.

PLAN1.md:626 advice Session discovery: by100 method text limitation :: by100 wraps `Method.text_closure`, which apparently doesn't accept compound method text like `(simp add: foo)` or `(metis lemma1 lemma2)`. Only bare method names work: `by (by100 simp)`, `by (by100 blast)`, `by (by100 auto)`, `by (by100 metis)`, etc. For specific lemma injections, use `using foo` + bare by100 method.
PLAN1.md:632 advice Session discovery: by100 method text limitation :: Workaround patterns: - `using facts by (by100 simp)` ‚Äî puts facts in simp's `using` rules. - Pre-derive intermediate facts by explicit rule application: `have h: "..." by (rule specific_lemma[OF ...])` (outside by100). - For algebra: use `by (by100 argo)` for real-arithmetic-closed conjectures.
PLAN1.md:638 advice Session discovery: by100 method text limitation :: This constraint shaped the endpoint-matching helper proof style ‚Äî all algebraic manipulations had to use explicit rule application + by100 simp.

PLAN1.md:1308 advice Barycentric decomposition fully formalized :: Classical algorithm (~920 lines) for x ‚àà œÉ ‚àà K: - Sort V (simplex vertices) descending by Œ± via `sort_key (Œªv. -Œ± v)`. - Build chain œÉ_seq k = conv hull (first k+1 elements), each face of œÉ. - Œ≤_k = (Suc k) * (Œ±_k - Œ±_{k+1}) with Œ±_{n+1} := 0. - Verify Œ≤ ‚â• 0, sum = 1 (telescoping), combo = x (swap + telescope). - Flag c = map œÉ_seq [0..<n]; c ‚àà flags. - W = bary ` set c, bij bary ‚àò œÉ_seq on {..<n}. - Define Œ≥ via inverse; apply convex_hull_finite to conclude x ‚àà conv hull W ‚àà bK.
PLAN1.md:1319 advice Key technical moves :: - `sort_key` with real-valued key works on arbitrary 'a (no linorder on 'a needed). - `sum.swap_restrict` for triangular swap of inner/outer indices. - `sum_Suc_diff'` applied to -Œ±' for reverse telescoping. - `set_take_subset_set_take` + card argument for strict-subset chains. - scaleR_cancel_right workaround: prove scalar equality, apply via sum.cong[of ...] + blast.

PLAN2.md:264 advice ADDENDUM 2026-04-25 (continued analysis): Session A sub-decomposition :: After repeated review, the "vertices concentrate in K'-simplex" lemma needs explicit decomposition. Here is the sub-strategy.
PLAN2.md:269 advice Why naive Lebesgue + open star fails :: For x near a K'-vertex v inside a K-simplex œÉ_K, the open star of v in K' restricted to œÉ_K is NOT covered by a single K'-simplex containing v. Multiple K'-simplices share v, and a small simplex œÑ around v may have vertices in different ones with no upper bound in K'. The counterexample is the "small disk on K'-vertex" case.
PLAN2.md:277 advice Why barycentric-chain structure of Sd^m K rescues this :: A vertex of Sd^m K is a barycenter b(œÉ_0...œÉ_m) for a chain œÉ_0 < ... < œÉ_m in K (actually m levels deep after m subdivisions). The KEY structural property: any TWO vertices of an Sd^m K simplex œÑ correspond to chains œÉ^A, œÉ^B that share a common chain TOP (the K-carrier of œÑ is determined by the top of all vertex-chains).
PLAN2.md:285 advice Sub-claim (Session A.1): chain-tops align :: For œÑ a face of Sd^m K with vertices b_0, ..., b_k, let chain_i be the K-flag whose barycenter is b_i. The K-carrier of œÑ is max_i top(chain_i), where top is the maximum K-simplex in the flag.
PLAN2.md:291 advice Sub-claim (Session A.2): vertex containment :: If x_i ‚àà œÉ_K is a barycenter from chain_i ‚äÜ œÉ_K (for all i), then x_i all share the SAME K-carrier œÉ_K. Moreover, for m large the chains chain_i differ only in their top portions, all bounded inside œÉ_K.
PLAN2.md:297 advice Sub-claim (Session A.3): K'-carrier match :: Given x_i all in œÉ_K with chain-tops aligned, for m large all x_i have the SAME K'-carrier œÉ' ‚äÜ œÉ_K. Hence œÑ = conv hull {x_0, ..., x_k} ‚äÜ œÉ' (by convexity).
PLAN2.md:303 advice Why this is harder than it looks :: Sub-claim A.3 is where the actual analytic work lies. It requires quantitative control over how Sd-iteration shrinks chains relative to K' vertex spacing. The standard Munkres argument uses the fact that mesh(Sd^m K) ‚Üí 0 (already proven via geotop_mesh_iterated_Sd) plus a specific Lebesgue number for K' relative to œÉ_K (a per-simplex quantity).
PLAN2.md:311 advice Direct alternative: bypass via diameter-ratio recursion :: Munkres-style proofs of refinement-by-mesh use a more subtle observation: for a simplex œÑ ‚äÜ K-simplex œÉ_K, if œÑ has its MIN vertex (in chain order) in rel_interior of some K'-simplex œÉ' ‚äÜ œÉ_K, and œÑ has diameter less than the rel-distance from rel_interior œÉ' to its rel_frontier, then œÑ ‚äÜ œÉ'.
PLAN2.md:317 advice Direct alternative: bypass via diameter-ratio recursion :: This requires per-K'-simplex rel-distance lower bounds (positive due to finite K' and compact K'-simplices), which is provable but adds ~150 lines of analytic infrastructure.
PLAN2.md:323 advice Recommendation :: Session A is genuinely deep classical work (~500-800 lines total). It should be attempted in a fresh focused session with no other competing work. The current state (sorry at line 9985 with full documentation; all bridge infrastructure in place) is a stable checkpoint suitable for handoff or pause.
PLAN2.md:331 advice NEW INFRASTRUCTURE 2026-04-25 (post-bridge-stabilization session) :: A 21-piece carrier-map infrastructure layer has been added on top of the bridge lemma. All commits green-built. Files: `b0/GeoTopBase0.thy`.

PLAN2.md:375 advice How this maps to N+2 reformulation :: For an Sd^m K-simplex œÑ with chain œÉ_0 ‚ää ... ‚ää œÉ_p in K: - Each Sd-vertex b_i = barycenter of some sub-flag of K - K_carrier(b_i) = chain-top of that sub-flag (via K_carrier_chain_combo) - For any positive convex-combination interior point x ‚àà rel_interior œÑ, K_carrier(x) = max chain-top across positive contributors - The bridge gives: K'_carrier(x) ‚äÜ K_carrier(x) - Refinement claim: for m large, K'_carrier(x) is constant on V_œÑ
PLAN2.md:383 advice How this maps to N+2 reformulation :: The refinement claim (constancy on V_œÑ as m ‚Üí ‚àû) is the remaining Sd-vertex-concentration argument. The infrastructure to STATE and manipulate it is now all in place.
PLAN3.md:5 advice SUPERSEDED ‚Äî see ANSWER_REPORT.md and ACK_ANSWER_REPORT.md :: The original PLAN3 (and PLAN3_WEEK1, PLAN3_WEEK2) followed the strategy of proving an iterated barycentric subdivision refinement theorem:
PLAN3.md:7 advice SUPERSEDED ‚Äî see ANSWER_REPORT.md and ACK_ANSWER_REPORT.md :: > Sd^N K refines K' for some N when K' is a subdivision of K.
PLAN3.md:9 advice SUPERSEDED ‚Äî see ANSWER_REPORT.md and ACK_ANSWER_REPORT.md :: **This claim is FALSE.** A 1-dimensional counterexample (subdivision of [0,1] at 1/3, dyadic-only Sd^N vertices) decisively disproves it. See `ANSWER_REPORT.md` for the mathematician's full analysis.
PLAN3.md:11 advice SUPERSEDED ‚Äî see ANSWER_REPORT.md and ACK_ANSWER_REPORT.md :: The correct strategy is the classical **overlay-cell-complex construction** (Hudson, *Piecewise Linear Topology*, Chapter I ¬ß2). This is documented in `ANSWER_REPORT.md` with an 8-phase Isabelle development plan.

PLAN3.md:25 advice Overview :: ```text Given finite simplicial complexes L1, L2 with |L1| = |L2|: 1. Form the raw overlay cells {œÉ ‚à© œÑ : œÉ ‚àà L1, œÑ ‚àà L2, œÉ ‚à© œÑ ‚âÝ ‚àÖ}. 2. Close under faces to get a finite convex cell complex C. 3. Triangulate C (Hudson Lemma 1.4 OR order-complex triangulation). 4. The triangulation M is the common subdivision: every simplex of M is contained in œÉ ‚à© œÑ for some œÉ ‚àà L1 and œÑ ‚àà L2. ```
PLAN3.md:34 advice Overview :: The new vertices created at boundaries of L1 ‚à™ L2 (e.g., at 1/3 in the counterexample) avoid the dyadic-vs-irrational issue.

PLAN3.md:52 advice Recommendations from mathematician :: 1. **Use face-closure approach** (don't require all cell faces present in original overlay). Add face closure as a definition step. Safer for formalization.
PLAN3.md:54 advice Recommendations from mathematician :: 2. **Use Option B (order-complex triangulation)** for Phase 5. Leverages our existing barycentric subdivision machinery (W1.0-W1.5 from previous work). Vertices = barycenters of cells, simplices = chains of cells under proper-face order.
PLAN3.md:56 advice Recommendations from mathematician :: 3. **Watch the union condition** carefully. "Every coarse simplex = finite union of finer simplices" is NOT automatic from containment + finiteness. Requires face-compatible triangulation, which is given by face-closure of overlay + cell-by-cell triangulation.
PLAN3.md:58 advice Recommendations from mathematician :: 4. **Hyperplane arrangement** as fallback. If overlay intersection property gets hard, switch to representing cells as sign/equality patterns over a finite set of supporting hyperplanes.
