PLAN3_PHASE1.md:34 advice Hudson's cell definition :: From Hudson Chapter I ¬ß2 (line 108-112):
PLAN3_PHASE1.md:36 advice Hudson's cell definition :: > (1) A cell is convex. Moreover, it is the convex hull of its vertices. > (2) The intersection and product of cells are cells. > (3) The convex hull of a finite set is a cell. > (4) Let A ‚äÜ E^p be a cell. Let f: E^p ‚Üí E^q be (affine) linear. Then f(A) is a cell.
PLAN3_PHASE1.md:41 advice Hudson's cell definition :: Hudson assumes (1)-(4) and a halfspace characterization of faces. For our purposes (Option B from the mathematician), we use the **convex-hull characterization** as the primary definition, since this matches our existing simplex definition (`geotop_is_simplex œÉ ‚ü∑ ‚àÉV. ... ‚àß œÉ = geotop_convex_hull V`).

PLAN3_PHASE1.md:113 advice Step 1.4: `geotop_cell_intersection` (~80-100 lines, the substantive part) :: ```isabelle lemma geotop_cell_intersection: fixes A B :: "'a::euclidean_space set" assumes hA: "geotop_cell A" assumes hB: "geotop_cell B" assumes h_int_ne: "A ‚à© B ‚âÝ {}" shows "geotop_cell (A ‚à© B)" ```
PLAN3_PHASE1.md:122 advice Step 1.4: `geotop_cell_intersection` (~80-100 lines, the substantive part) :: **Approach:** the intersection of two convex hulls of finite sets is again a polytope (this is a theorem in convex geometry, Minkowski-Weyl). Specifically: - Both A and B are bounded convex polyhedra (intersections of finitely many halfspaces). - A ‚à© B is also a bounded convex polyhedron. - A ‚à© B has finitely many vertices, and equals the convex hull of those vertices.
PLAN3_PHASE1.md:127 advice Step 1.4: `geotop_cell_intersection` (~80-100 lines, the substantive part) :: **HOL-Analysis support:** this should exist as `polytope_Int` or similar. If not, we'll need to prove it.
PLAN3_PHASE1.md:129 advice Step 1.4: `geotop_cell_intersection` (~80-100 lines, the substantive part) :: **Strategy:** 1. First check HOL-Analysis for `polytope_Int`. If exists, reuse directly. 2. If not, prove via halfspace representation: - A = ‚ãÇ halfspaces, B = ‚ãÇ halfspaces (both finite) - A ‚à© B = ‚ãÇ all those halfspaces (still finite) - A ‚à© B is a polyhedron (closed convex) - A ‚à© B is bounded (subset of A) - Bounded polyhedron = polytope = convex hull of vertices
PLAN3_PHASE1.md:138 advice Step 1.4: `geotop_cell_intersection` (~80-100 lines, the substantive part) :: **Risk:** medium. Could be ~30 lines if HOL-Analysis has `polytope_Int`, ~100+ lines otherwise.

PLAN3_PHASE2.md:38 advice Hudson's face definition :: From Hudson Chapter I appendix (lines 653-668):
PLAN3_PHASE2.md:40 advice Hudson's face definition :: > B is a face of cell A iff: > (1) P ‚à© A = B, where P is the hyperplane spanned by B. > (2) No point of P lies between any two points of A ‚àí B.
PLAN3_PHASE2.md:44 advice Hudson's face definition :: This is the **exposed-face / supporting-hyperplane** characterization. Equivalent to HOL-Analysis's `face_of` for closed convex sets in finite-dimensional Euclidean space.
PLAN3_PHASE2.md:46 advice Hudson's face definition :: **Pragmatic strategy:** use HOL-Analysis `face_of` directly. Don't re-derive Hudson's halfspace characterization unless required.
PLAN3_PHASE2.md:48 advice Hudson's face definition :: ```isabelle definition geotop_cell_face :: "'a::euclidean_space set ‚áí 'a set ‚áí bool" where "geotop_cell_face F C ‚ü∑ geotop_cell C ‚àß F ‚âÝ {} ‚àß F face_of C" ```
PLAN3_PHASE2.md:53 advice Hudson's face definition :: **Note**: `F ‚âÝ {}` is a stylistic choice. Hudson allows the empty face implicitly via the empty cell. In our formalization we exclude the empty cell (Phase 1 def requires `C ‚âÝ {}`), so faces are also non-empty.

PLAN3_PHASE2.md:102 advice Step 2.4: Face is a cell (~30 lines) :: **Approach:** - F is a face of C, so F is a closed convex subset of C (HOL-Analysis: face_of_imp_compact when C compact). - F is a polytope (face of polytope is polytope ‚Äî HOL-Analysis: `polytope_face_of_polytope` or similar). - Polytope = convex hull of finite set (HOL-Analysis: `polytope_def`). - Therefore F = convex hull of finite set, hence a geotop_cell.
PLAN3_PHASE2.md:108 advice Step 2.4: Face is a cell (~30 lines) :: **HOL-Analysis support to look for:** - `polytope_face_of_polytope` - `polytope_def` (equivalent to "convex hull of finite set" for compact convex sets)

PLAN3_PHASE2.md:123 advice Step 2.5: Transitivity (~10 lines) :: **Approach:** uses HOL-Analysis `face_of_trans`: face of face is face. Plus F is a cell from 2.4.

PLAN3_PHASE2.md:154 advice Step 2.7: Simplex faces are cell faces (~10 lines) :: **Approach:** - œÉ and œÑ are both simplices (geotop_is_face hypothesis ‚üπ œÑ is simplex). - œÉ is a cell (Phase 1 Step 1.3). - œÑ ‚äÜ œÉ and œÑ is a "geometric" face of œÉ in the simplicial sense. - Need: œÑ face_of œÉ in HOL-Analysis sense.

PLAN3_PHASE3.md:27 advice Hudson's definitions :: From Hudson Chapter I ¬ß2 (lines 148-164):
PLAN3_PHASE3.md:29 advice Hudson's definitions :: > A **convex linear cell complex** in `E^n` is a finite set of cells in `E^n`, K, such that > 1. If A ‚àà K, every face of A is in K. > 2. If A and B ‚àà K, then A ‚à© B = ‚àÖ or A ‚à© B = common face of A and B.
PLAN3_PHASE3.md:33 advice Hudson's definitions :: > A **simplicial complex** is a cell complex whose cells are all simplices.
PLAN3_PHASE3.md:35 advice Hudson's definitions :: > If K and L are cell complexes, K is called a **subdivision** of L if: > 1. |K| = |L|. > 2. Every cell of K is a subset of some cell of L.
PLAN3_PHASE3.md:39 advice Hudson's definitions :: (Hudson Lemma 1.2 derives the converse "every cell of L is a union of cells of K", so this is equivalent to Munkres' two-clause definition.)
PLAN3_PHASE3.md:41 advice Hudson's definitions :: ---
PLAN3_PHASE3.md:47 advice Step 3.1: Define `geotop_cell_complex` (~10 lines) :: ```isabelle definition geotop_cell_complex :: "'a::euclidean_space set set ‚áí bool" where "geotop_cell_complex C ‚ü∑ finite C ‚àß (‚àÄA‚ààC. geotop_cell A) ‚àß (‚àÄA‚ààC. ‚àÄF. geotop_cell_face F A ‚ü∂ F ‚àà C) ‚àß (‚àÄA‚ààC. ‚àÄB‚ààC. A ‚à© B = {} ‚à® (geotop_cell_face (A ‚à© B) A ‚àß geotop_cell_face (A ‚à© B) B))" ```

A ‚äÜ B)" ```

PLAN3_PHASE3.md:83 advice Step 3.4: Bridge ‚Äî simplicial complex is a cell complex (~30 lines) :: ```isabelle lemma geotop_simplicial_complex_imp_cell_complex: fixes K :: "'a::euclidean_space set set" assumes hK: "geotop_is_complex K" assumes hKfin: "finite K" shows "geotop_cell_complex K" ```
PLAN3_PHASE3.md:91 advice Step 3.4: Bridge ‚Äî simplicial complex is a cell complex (~30 lines) :: **Approach:** - K is finite (assumption). - Each œÉ ‚àà K is a simplex, hence a cell (Phase 1 Step 1.3). - Each face of œÉ ‚àà K is in K (geotop_is_complex K.1 axiom). - Each pairwise intersection œÉ ‚à© œÑ ‚àà K is a common face (K.2 axiom). - The "common face" in the simplicial sense gives the cell-face condition (Phase 2 Step 2.7 ‚Äî simplex_face_imp_cell_face).
PLAN3_PHASE3.md:98 advice Step 3.4: Bridge ‚Äî simplicial complex is a cell complex (~30 lines) :: **Risk:** medium. The bridge from K.2 (simplex ‚à© simplex = simplex face) to cell-face requires careful argument.
PLAN3_PHASE3.md:102 advice Step 3.5: Polyhedron coincidence (~5 lines) :: ```isabelle lemma geotop_simplicial_polyhedron_eq_cell_polyhedron: fixes K :: "'a::euclidean_space set set" shows "geotop_polyhedron K = geotop_cell_polyhedron K" unfolding geotop_polyhedron_def geotop_cell_polyhedron_def by simp ```
PLAN3_PHASE3.md:109 advice Step 3.5: Polyhedron coincidence (~5 lines) :: (Trivially true since both = ‚ãÉ K.)
PLAN3_PHASE3.md:111 advice Step 3.5: Polyhedron coincidence (~5 lines) :: **Risk:** very low.
PLAN3_PHASE3.md:115 advice Step 3.6: Subdivision bridge (~20 lines) :: ```isabelle lemma geotop_simplicial_subdivision_imp_cell_subdivision: fixes K K' :: "'a::euclidean_space set set" assumes hK_subdiv: "geotop_is_subdivision K' K" assumes hKfin: "finite K" shows "geotop_cell_is_subdivision K' K" ```
PLAN3_PHASE3.md:123 advice Step 3.6: Subdivision bridge (~20 lines) :: **Approach:** - Both K, K' are finite simplicial complexes. - By 3.4, both are cell complexes. - |K| = |K'| (subdivision property). - Every K'-simplex ‚äÜ some K-simplex (subdivision property). - Hence cell_is_subdivision.
PLAN3_PHASE3.md:130 advice Step 3.6: Subdivision bridge (~20 lines) :: **Risk:** low.

PLAN3_PHASE4.md:32 advice Key strategy from mathematician :: > ### Strong recommendation > Use **face closure**. It is safer for formalization. > > Then the overlay definition becomes: > ```isabelle > definition geotop_overlay_complex where > "geotop_overlay_complex L1 L2 = > geotop_face_closure (geotop_overlay_cells L1 L2)" > ``` > Now the closure-under-faces property is by construction.
PLAN3_PHASE4.md:43 advice Key strategy from mathematician :: This avoids the difficulty of proving "every face of œÉ ‚à© œÑ is itself an overlay cell" (which Hudson's original definition would require).

PLAN3_PHASE4.md:136 advice Step 4.7: Raw cells refine left and right (~5 lines each) :: **Risk:** very low. Direct from definition.
PLAN3_PHASE4.md:140 advice Step 4.8: Overlay complex finite (~20 lines) :: ```isabelle lemma geotop_overlay_complex_finite: assumes "geotop_is_complex L1" "geotop_is_complex L2" assumes "finite L1" "finite L2" shows "finite (geotop_overlay_complex L1 L2)" ```
PLAN3_PHASE4.md:147 advice Step 4.8: Overlay complex finite (~20 lines) :: **Approach:** - Raw cells are finite (Step 4.5). - Each raw cell is a cell, hence has finitely many faces (Phase 2 Step 2.6). - Face closure of a finite set of cells with finitely many faces each is finite.
PLAN3_PHASE4.md:152 advice Step 4.8: Overlay complex finite (~20 lines) :: **Risk:** medium. Need careful counting.
PLAN3_PHASE4.md:156 advice Step 4.9: Each face-closure element is a cell (~15 lines) :: ```isabelle lemma geotop_overlay_complex_elt_is_cell: assumes "geotop_is_complex L1" "geotop_is_complex L2" assumes "finite L1" "finite L2" assumes "F ‚àà geotop_overlay_complex L1 L2" shows "geotop_cell F" ```
PLAN3_PHASE4.md:164 advice Step 4.9: Each face-closure element is a cell (~15 lines) :: **Approach:** F is a face of some raw cell C. Raw cell is a cell (Step 4.4). Face of a cell is a cell (Phase 2 Step 2.4).
PLAN3_PHASE4.md:166 advice Step 4.9: Each face-closure element is a cell (~15 lines) :: **Risk:** low.
PLAN3_PHASE4.md:170 advice Step 4.10: Overlay complex closed under faces (~10 lines) :: ```isabelle lemma geotop_overlay_complex_closed_under_faces: assumes "F ‚àà geotop_overlay_complex L1 L2" assumes "geotop_cell_face G F" shows "G ‚àà geotop_overlay_complex L1 L2" ```
PLAN3_PHASE4.md:177 advice Step 4.10: Overlay complex closed under faces (~10 lines) :: **Approach:** F is a face of some raw cell C. G is a face of F. By transitivity (Phase 2 Step 2.5), G is a face of C. Hence G ‚àà face_closure(raw_cells).
PLAN3_PHASE4.md:179 advice Step 4.10: Overlay complex closed under faces (~10 lines) :: **Risk:** low.
PLAN3_PHASE4.md:183 advice Step 4.11: Pairwise intersection is common face (~30 lines, the substantive part) :: ```isabelle lemma geotop_overlay_complex_pairwise_face: assumes "geotop_is_complex L1" "geotop_is_complex L2" assumes "F1 ‚àà geotop_overlay_complex L1 L2" assumes "F2 ‚àà geotop_overlay_complex L1 L2" assumes "F1 ‚à© F2 ‚âÝ {}" shows "geotop_cell_face (F1 ‚à© F2) F1 ‚àß geotop_cell_face (F1 ‚à© F2) F2" ```
PLAN3_PHASE4.md:192 advice Step 4.11: Pairwise intersection is common face (~30 lines, the substantive part) :: **Approach (per mathematician's note in ¬ß16):** - F1 is a face of raw cell `œÉ1 ‚à© œÑ1`, F2 is a face of raw cell `œÉ2 ‚à© œÑ2`. - F1 ‚à© F2 is a face of `(œÉ1 ‚à© œÑ1) ‚à© (œÉ2 ‚à© œÑ2) = (œÉ1 ‚à© œÉ2) ‚à© (œÑ1 ‚à© œÑ2)`. - Since L1, L2 are simplicial complexes, `œÉ1 ‚à© œÉ2` is empty or common face, similarly `œÑ1 ‚à© œÑ2`. - Hence the intersection is well-structured and F1 ‚à© F2 is a common face.
PLAN3_PHASE4.md:198 advice Step 4.11: Pairwise intersection is common face (~30 lines, the substantive part) :: **Risk:** medium-high. This is the geometric core of the overlay construction.
PLAN3_PHASE4.md:202 advice Step 4.12: Overlay complex is a cell complex (~15 lines) :: ```isabelle theorem geotop_overlay_complex_is_cell_complex: assumes "geotop_is_complex L1" "geotop_is_complex L2" assumes "finite L1" "finite L2" shows "geotop_cell_complex (geotop_overlay_complex L1 L2)" ```
PLAN3_PHASE4.md:209 advice Step 4.12: Overlay complex is a cell complex (~15 lines) :: **Approach:** combine 4.8 (finite) + 4.9 (elements are cells) + 4.10 (closed under faces) + 4.11 (pairwise face intersection).
PLAN3_PHASE4.md:211 advice Step 4.12: Overlay complex is a cell complex (~15 lines) :: **Risk:** low if 4.11 succeeds.
PLAN3_PHASE4.md:215 advice Step 4.13: Polyhedron and refinement properties (~30 lines) :: ```isabelle lemma geotop_overlay_complex_union: assumes "geotop_polyhedron L1 = geotop_polyhedron L2" shows "geotop_cell_polyhedron (geotop_overlay_complex L1 L2) = geotop_polyhedron L1"
PLAN3_PHASE4.md:220 advice Step 4.13: Polyhedron and refinement properties (~30 lines) :: lemma geotop_overlay_complex_refines_left: assumes "F ‚àà geotop_overlay_complex L1 L2" shows "‚àÉœÉ‚ààL1. F ‚äÜ œÉ"
PLAN3_PHASE4.md:224 advice Step 4.13: Polyhedron and refinement properties (~30 lines) :: lemma geotop_overlay_complex_refines_right: assumes "F ‚àà geotop_overlay_complex L1 L2" shows "‚àÉœÑ‚ààL2. F ‚äÜ œÑ" ```
PLAN3_PHASE4.md:229 advice Step 4.13: Polyhedron and refinement properties (~30 lines) :: **Approach:** - Polyhedron: face closure of raw cells covers what raw cells cover; raw cells cover common polyhedron (Step 4.6). - Refinement: F is a face of raw cell C; C ‚äÜ œÉ ‚àà L1 (Step 4.7); F ‚äÜ C ‚äÜ œÉ.
PLAN3_PHASE4.md:233 advice Step 4.13: Polyhedron and refinement properties (~30 lines) :: **Risk:** low.
PLAN3_PHASE4.md:237 advice Step 4.14: Each L1-simplex is a union of overlay cells (~30 lines) :: ```isabelle lemma geotop_overlay_complex_covers_each_left_simplex: assumes "œÉ ‚àà L1" assumes "geotop_polyhedron L1 = geotop_polyhedron L2" shows "œÉ = ‚ãÉ {A ‚àà geotop_overlay_complex L1 L2. A ‚äÜ œÉ}" ```
PLAN3_PHASE4.md:244 advice Step 4.14: Each L1-simplex is a union of overlay cells (~30 lines) :: (And analogous for L2.)
PLAN3_PHASE4.md:246 advice Step 4.14: Each L1-simplex is a union of overlay cells (~30 lines) :: **Approach (per mathematician's ¬ß27):** - œÉ = ‚ãÉ overlay_cells contained in œÉ (since L1, L2 partition œÉ via simplicial intersections). - Each overlay cell is also in face-closed overlay.
PLAN3_PHASE4.md:250 advice Step 4.14: Each L1-simplex is a union of overlay cells (~30 lines) :: **Risk:** medium. Subtle but essential for the union condition in Phase 6.

PLAN3_PHASE5.md:15 advice Hudson Lemma 1.4 :: From Hudson Chapter I ¬ß3 (lines 262-271):
PLAN3_PHASE5.md:17 advice Hudson Lemma 1.4 :: > **Lemma 1.4.** If K is a cell complex, then K has a simplicial subdivision with no extra vertices. > > **Proof.** Order the vertices of K. If A ‚àà K, write A = a ¬∑ B, a the first vertex of A, B = all faces of A not containing a. Define subdivision of cells in order of increasing dimension by the rule: > > A' = a ¬∑ B' > > where B' is the subdivision of B determined by the (simplicial) subdivision of cells of lower dimension. (If A = a, set A' = a). The construction is self-consistent because if C is a face of A containing a, then a is the first vertex of C.
PLAN3_PHASE5.md:25 advice Hudson Lemma 1.4 :: So Hudson uses **induction by dimension**, choosing a distinguished "first vertex" (under a global ordering) for each cell, and coning from that vertex over the already-triangulated boundary.
PLAN3_PHASE5.md:27 advice Hudson Lemma 1.4 :: ---
PLAN3_PHASE5.md:33 advice Option A: Hudson Lemma 1.4 directly :: Pros: follows the textbook exactly. Cons: requires global vertex ordering, induction over cells by dimension, and several supporting lemmas about cone constructions.
PLAN3_PHASE5.md:38 advice Option B: Order-complex / barycentric subdivision of cell poset :: Construct a simplicial complex M whose: - Vertices are barycenters of cells. - Simplices are convex hulls of `{barycenter(A_0), barycenter(A_1), ..., barycenter(A_k)}` where `A_0 ‚ää A_1 ‚ää ... ‚ää A_k` is a chain of cells under proper-face order.
PLAN3_PHASE5.md:42 advice Option B: Order-complex / barycentric subdivision of cell poset :: Pros: very canonical, uniform; leverages existing `geotop_chain_simplex_vertices_in_top` etc. infrastructure (Week 1 work + carrier-map machinery). Cons: need to prove affine independence of cell barycenters in chains, plus coverage.
PLAN3_PHASE5.md:47 advice Recommendation (per mathematician) :: > If your current development already has strong barycentric subdivision machinery, Option B may be faster.
PLAN3_PHASE5.md:49 advice Recommendation (per mathematician) :: We have **substantial** barycentric infrastructure (28+ exports, including `geotop_chain_simplex_vertices_in_top`, `geotop_K_carriers_of_barycenters`). **Choose Option B.**
PLAN3_PHASE5.md:51 advice Recommendation (per mathematician) :: ---

PLAN3_PHASE5.md:120 advice Step 5.6: Order complex is a simplicial complex (~50 lines) :: **Approach:** - K.0 (each element is a simplex): from chain-simplex def + 5.3 + 5.4. - K.1 (closed under faces): face of chain-simplex corresponds to sub-chain. - K.2 (pairwise intersection is a face): non-trivial; uses cell complex pairwise intersection. - K.3 (locally finite): trivial since order complex is finite.

PLAN3_PHASE5.md:136 advice Step 5.7: Polyhedron equality (~40 lines) :: **Approach:** ‚äÜ direct (each chain-simplex is in some chain-top cell). ‚äá: for each x ‚àà |C|, x lies in some cell A; barycenter chain ending at A's K-carrier in C gives a chain-simplex containing x. Uses inductive construction.

PLAN3_PHASE5.md:142 advice Step 5.8: Order complex refines C (~15 lines) :: ```isabelle theorem geotop_order_complex_refines: assumes "geotop_cell_complex C" assumes "S ‚àà geotop_order_complex C" shows "‚àÉA ‚àà C. S ‚äÜ A" ```
PLAN3_PHASE5.md:149 advice Step 5.8: Order complex refines C (~15 lines) :: **Approach:** S = chain-simplex of c. Take A = last c (chain top). barycenters of c ‚äÜ A (analog of `geotop_chain_simplex_vertices_in_top` for cells). conv hull ‚äÜ A by convexity.
PLAN3_PHASE5.md:151 advice Step 5.8: Order complex refines C (~15 lines) :: **Risk:** low. Mirrors existing simplex argument.

PLAN3_PHASE5.md:162 advice Step 5.9: Cell-by-cell coverage (induced subdivisions) (~30 lines) :: **Approach:** ‚äá direct. ‚äÜ: for each x ‚àà A, find a chain-simplex containing x with A as chain-top. Uses the same inductive argument as 5.7.

PLAN3_PHASE6.md:43 advice Step 6.1: General bridge lemma (~50 lines) :: ```isabelle lemma geotop_simplicial_subdivision_from_cell_subdivision: fixes M C :: "'a::euclidean_space set set" fixes L :: "'a::euclidean_space set set" assumes hL: "geotop_is_complex L" assumes hLfin: "finite L" assumes hM_simp: "geotop_is_complex M" assumes hMfin: "finite M" assumes hC_cell: "geotop_cell_complex C" assumes hMC: "geotop_cell_is_subdivision M C" assumes h_C_in_L: "‚àÄA ‚àà C. ‚àÉœÉ ‚àà L. A ‚äÜ œÉ" assumes h_L_covered: "‚àÄœÉ ‚àà L. œÉ = ‚ãÉ {A ‚àà C. A ‚äÜ œÉ}" assumes h_M_induced: "‚àÄA ‚àà C. A = ‚ãÉ {œÅ ‚àà M. œÅ ‚äÜ A}" shows "geotop_is_subdivision M L" ```
PLAN3_PHASE6.md:59 advice Step 6.1: General bridge lemma (~50 lines) :: **Approach:** - Need to show: (a) M is a complex (given by hM_simp). (b) L is a complex (given by hL). (c) `geotop_polyhedron M = geotop_polyhedron L`. (d) `‚àÄœÅ ‚àà M. ‚àÉœÉ ‚àà L. œÅ ‚äÜ œÉ` (the refinement / Munkres clause 1).
PLAN3_PHASE6.md:66 advice Step 6.1: General bridge lemma (~50 lines) :: For (c): polyhedron of M = polyhedron of C (cell subdivision) = polyhedron of L (chain via h_L_covered).
PLAN3_PHASE6.md:68 advice Step 6.1: General bridge lemma (~50 lines) :: For (d): œÅ ‚àà M ‚üπ œÅ lies in some cell A ‚àà C (cell subdivision); A ‚äÜ œÉ for some œÉ ‚àà L (h_C_in_L); transitively œÅ ‚äÜ œÉ.
PLAN3_PHASE6.md:70 advice Step 6.1: General bridge lemma (~50 lines) :: Note: our `geotop_is_subdivision` definition (in `b0/GeoTopBase0.thy`) requires: - both complexes - |M| = |L| - geotop_refines M L (which is `‚àÄœÅ ‚àà M. ‚àÉœÉ ‚àà L. œÅ ‚äÜ œÉ`)
PLAN3_PHASE6.md:75 advice Step 6.1: General bridge lemma (~50 lines) :: The Munkres "every L-simplex is finite union of M-simplices" is implied by the polyhedron-equality + refinement + finiteness combination, which we have via h_L_covered + h_M_induced.

PLAN3_WEEK2.md:28 advice ‚öÝ CRITICAL FINDING (2026-04-26 fresh-session setup) :: **Setup analysis revealed W2.1 as stated is unprovable via rel-distance.**
PLAN3_WEEK2.md:30 advice ‚öÝ CRITICAL FINDING (2026-04-26 fresh-session setup) :: Counterexample: œÉ_K = triangle [a,b,c], œÉ' = edge [a,b] (a FACE of œÉ_K, V_œÉ' = {a,b} ‚äÜ V_œÉ_K = {a,b,c}). Take x = midpoint of [a,b] ‚àà rel_interior œÉ'. Points in œÉ_K \ œÉ' (triangle interior) can be arbitrarily close to x. So dist(x, œÉ_K \ œÉ') = 0. Same for œÉ_K \ rel_interior œÉ'.
PLAN3_WEEK2.md:32 advice ‚öÝ CRITICAL FINDING (2026-04-26 fresh-session setup) :: **Conclusion:** œÉ_K \ œÉ' (or \ rel_interior œÉ') is NOT closed for œÉ' a face of œÉ_K when dim œÉ' < dim œÉ_K. The rel-distance approach fundamentally cannot work this way.
PLAN3_WEEK2.md:34 advice ‚öÝ CRITICAL FINDING (2026-04-26 fresh-session setup) :: **Implication for Week 2 plan:** the Munkres ¬ß16 argument must use a DIFFERENT analytic technique. The W2.1 + W2.3 structure as drafted is unworkable.
PLAN3_WEEK2.md:38 advice Revised approach (Munkres ¬ß16 "open star" argument) :: The classical proof does NOT use rel-distance bounds. Instead:
PLAN3_WEEK2.md:40 advice Revised approach (Munkres ¬ß16 "open star" argument) :: 1. Lebesgue Œµ for K'-vertex-star cover (already proven in `iterated_Sd_refines_subdivision`). 2. For œÑ ‚äÜ open_star(v, K') (one K'-vertex v), each w ‚àà V_œÑ has v ‚àà V(K'-carrier(w)). 3. **Key new claim**: for œÑ a Sd-simplex (V_œÑ are barycenters of a K-flag chain), the K'-carriers œÉ'_i = K'-carrier(barycenter œÉ_i) for i = 0..p (chain) all contain v, AND they are NESTED: œÉ'_0 ‚äÜ œÉ'_1 ‚äÜ ... ‚äÜ œÉ'_p.
PLAN3_WEEK2.md:44 advice Revised approach (Munkres ¬ß16 "open star" argument) :: The nesting claim is the actual deep fact. It comes from: - œÉ'_i ‚äÜ œÉ_i ‚äÜ œÉ_p (chain). - v ‚àà œÉ'_i for each i (from open_star). - œÉ'_i ‚à© œÉ'_p contains v, hence non-empty. By K.2 axiom, œÉ'_i ‚à© œÉ'_p is a face of both. - For œÉ'_i ‚äÜ œÉ'_p, we need œÉ'_i = œÉ'_i ‚à© œÉ'_p, i.e., œÉ'_p ‚äá œÉ'_i.
PLAN3_WEEK2.md:50 advice Revised approach (Munkres ¬ß16 "open star" argument) :: The "œÉ'_p ‚äá œÉ'_i" claim requires œÉ'_p to be the K'-CARRIER of barycenter œÉ_p, which has DIMENSION = dim œÉ_p (since barycenter œÉ_p is in rel_interior œÉ_p, which is "deepest" in œÉ_p). The dim hierarchy might give œÉ'_i face of œÉ'_p.
PLAN3_WEEK2.md:52 advice Revised approach (Munkres ¬ß16 "open star" argument) :: **This is still a non-trivial claim.** Will need careful argument.
PLAN3_WEEK2.md:56 advice Revised W2 plan :: - **W2.1 (REVISED)**: K'-carrier dimension = K-carrier dimension when point in rel_interior of K-simplex (~80 lines). For x ‚àà rel_interior œÉ_K, the K'-carrier œÉ'_x has same dim as œÉ_K (or related dim relationship). - **W2.2**: K-flag chain elements form a face-chain in K (~60 lines). Same as before ‚Äî uses face axiom. - **W2.3 (REVISED)**: K'-carriers œÉ'_i along the K-flag chain are nested (~80 lines). The deep analytic claim. - **W2.4**: Discharge h_inter_ne (~50 lines). With nested K'-carriers, œÉ'_p contains all V_œÑ, giving Munkres 14.4 ‚üπ result.
PLAN3_WEEK2.md:63 advice Mathematical correctness check :: Before proceeding with W2.1 revised, need to verify the nesting claim itself by hand on a small example. The fresh session should START by working a 2D example:
PLAN3_WEEK2.md:65 advice Mathematical correctness check :: K = single triangle [a,b,c]. K' = some specific subdivision. For an Sd^1 K-simplex œÑ with K-flag c = [{a}, [a,b]] (a chain), V_œÑ = {barycenter {a}, barycenter [a,b]} = {a, midpoint ab}.
PLAN3_WEEK2.md:67 advice Mathematical correctness check :: K'-carriers: - K'-carrier(a) = some K'-simplex with a in its rel_interior. Since {a} ‚àà K' (subdivision preserves K-vertices), K'-carrier(a) = {a}. - K'-carrier(midpoint ab) = some K'-simplex with midpoint ab in its rel_interior. Depends on K' structure.
PLAN3_WEEK2.md:71 advice Mathematical correctness check :: For nesting: {a} ‚äÜ K'-carrier(midpoint ab)? This requires the K'-simplex containing midpoint ab in rel_interior to also contain a. Since a is on the boundary of K'-carrier(midpoint ab) (assuming the K'-simplex extends to include endpoint a), this is plausible.
PLAN3_WEEK2.md:73 advice Mathematical correctness check :: But for a K' that subdivides edge ab into many segments, K'-carrier(midpoint ab) might be a tiny segment NOT containing a. Then {a} ‚äÑ K'-carrier(midpoint ab), and the nesting claim FAILS.
PLAN3_WEEK2.md:75 advice Mathematical correctness check :: **This suggests the nesting claim is actually FALSE in general.** Need to think harder.
PLAN3_WEEK2.md:79 advice Revised conclusion :: The classical Munkres ¬ß16 argument is more subtle than direct nesting. Possible approaches:
PLAN3_WEEK2.md:81 advice Revised conclusion :: 1. Use ITERATION m large enough that ALL Sd^m K-vertices fall into the SAME K'-simplex. This requires a quantitative argument about how Sd-iteration "concentrates" vertices.
PLAN3_WEEK2.md:83 advice Revised conclusion :: 2. Use a more elaborate carrier-chain argument that doesn't require literal nesting but exploits Sd-flag-chain refinement.
PLAN3_WEEK2.md:85 advice Revised conclusion :: 3. Use Munkres' ¬ß16.4 direct subdivision-mapping argument (different proof path).
PLAN3_WEEK2.md:87 advice Revised conclusion :: Given the difficulty, Week 2 may need to expand to a 2-3 week effort, with W2 being preparatory and the actual h_inter_ne discharge happening in W3.

PLAN3_WEEK2.md:144 advice W2.2: Sd-flag chain top is a K-FACE not just sub-K-simplex (~60 lines) :: **Approach:** The K-flag c has elements œÉ_0 ‚ää œÉ_1 ‚ää ... ‚ää œÉ_p in K. By complex face axiom, œÉ_i ‚ää œÉ_j (both in K, œÉ_i ‚ää œÉ_j non-empty intersection) implies œÉ_i is a FACE of œÉ_j (V(œÉ_i) ‚äÜ V(œÉ_j)).

PLAN3_WEEK2.md:258 advice Critical mathematical insight (for the operator) :: The reason W2.1 is restricted to œÉ' a FACE of œÉ_K (V_œÉ' ‚äÜ V_œÉ_K) and not arbitrary K'-simplex:
PLAN3_WEEK2.md:260 advice Critical mathematical insight (for the operator) :: **Counterexample to the general statement** (from PLAN2 ADDENDUM): Take K = single 2-simplex, K' = subdivision adding interior vertex m + 6 sub-triangles. œÉ' = {m} (a 0-simplex of K' inside œÉ_K's interior). œÉ' is NOT a face of œÉ_K (m is not a K-vertex). œÉ_K \ {m} = open disk minus a point, NOT closed. dist(m, œÉ_K \ {m}) = 0.
PLAN3_WEEK2.md:263 advice Critical mathematical insight (for the operator) :: **The fix**: Sd-vertices are barycenters of K-simplices, and their K-CARRIERS are face-chain elements (W2.2). The K'-carriers œÉ'_i ‚äÜ œÉ_i (chain element), so œÉ'_i are sub-K'-simplices of K-FACES of œÉ_K. The face structure is preserved.
PLAN3_WEEK2.md:265 advice Critical mathematical insight (for the operator) :: **Munkres ¬ß16 essential idea**: Sd-simplices are "aligned" with K-faces via the chain structure. Subdivisions K' that don't preserve this face-alignment (like adding a new interior vertex m) are STILL OK ‚Äî because Sd^m K simplices for m large have V_œÑ K-carriers that nest into the K-face structure, and the K'-simplices œÉ'_i preserve this nesting via the bridge.
PLAN3_WEEK2.md:267 advice Critical mathematical insight (for the operator) :: The hard part of W2.3: making "K'-simplices preserve face nesting" precise.

PLAN4.md:185 advice 3. Workflow rules (verbatim from CLAUDE.md, restated for emphasis) :: * **Sorry-first**: any new proof step is `sorry` until a build pass; replace via sledgehammer in batches of 3‚Äì5; wrap every replacement with `by100`. * **Never collapse have/obtain scaffolding** to lower the sorry count. The scaffolding *is* the formal progress. * **Fix buggy defs immediately** when discovered; don't paper over. * **Stick to geotop.tex** structure of every proof. Deviate only when forced; document. * **Time watch**: keep build under 30 s of incremental time per phase; if it blows up, decompose the slowest by100 calls before adding new content. * **No `-c`, no `-f`** on the build command ‚Äî plain `isabelle build -D .`.

PLAN4_PHASE_D_LOG.md:26 advice The Jordan-polygon obstacle (GT_2_1) :: `Theorem_GT_2_1` ("‚Ñù¬≤ ‚àí J has exactly two components") factors through `Lemma_GT_2_1a` (‚â§ 2) and `Lemma_GT_2_1b` (‚â• 2). Both lemmas have direct sorries (the Moise scaffolding for the parity argument was never closed).
PLAN4_PHASE_D_LOG.md:30 advice The Jordan-polygon obstacle (GT_2_1) :: Reproving Moise's strip-neighborhood + Ind-P parity argument is a multi-week effort. The library route is HOL-Analysis's `Jordan_curve` (in `Jordan_Curve.thy:216`), which gives "exactly two components" for any simple closed path. **However**, `Jordan_curve` is stated for `real \<Rightarrow> complex` and its key dependency `Janiszewski` is `complex`-only. Both `Janiszewski` and `Janiszewski_weak` use cball/sphere norm bounds in a type-specific way that doesn't generalize trivially.
PLAN4_PHASE_D_LOG.md:38 advice The Jordan-polygon obstacle (GT_2_1) :: Three viable strategies, in increasing cost:
PLAN4_PHASE_D_LOG.md:42 advice Strategy A ‚Äî `real^2 \<leftrightarrow> complex` bridge (recommended) :: 1. Define `\<phi> :: real^2 \<Rightarrow> complex`, `\<phi> v = Complex (v $ 1) (v $ 2)`. 2. Define inverse `\<psi> :: complex \<Rightarrow> real^2`. 3. Show `\<phi>` is bijective, linear, and a homeomorphism (linear bijection between finite-dim normed spaces). 4. Build `Jordan_curve_real2`: given a polygon J in `real^2`, construct `simple_path (\<phi> \<circ> c)` in `complex`, apply `Jordan_curve`, transport the two components back via `\<psi>`. 5. From `geotop_is_polygon J` extract a parametrising `simple_path c :: real \<Rightarrow> real^2` with `path_image c = J` (uses `geotop_is_n_sphere` and the standard parametrisation of S¬π).
PLAN4_PHASE_D_LOG.md:53 advice Strategy A ‚Äî `real^2 \<leftrightarrow> complex` bridge (recommended) :: Estimated effort: 200‚Äì400 lines, 1‚Äì2 days.
PLAN4_PHASE_D_LOG.md:57 advice Strategy B ‚Äî Lift `Janiszewski` to `'a::euclidean_space` with `DIM('a) = 2` :: Replace `complex` by an abstract `'a::euclidean_space` constraint everywhere in `Janiszewski`, `Janiszewski_weak`, `Jordan_curve`, then specialise to `real^2`. Requires editing Isabelle proofs in HOL-Analysis files we'd rather not touch.
PLAN4_PHASE_D_LOG.md:61 advice Strategy B ‚Äî Lift `Janiszewski` to `'a::euclidean_space` with `DIM('a) = 2` :: Estimated effort: 1+ week, fragile.
PLAN4_PHASE_D_LOG.md:65 advice Strategy C ‚Äî Reprove Moise's parity argument :: Closest to the geotop.tex proof, but reinventing what HOL already has.
PLAN4_PHASE_D_LOG.md:67 advice Strategy C ‚Äî Reprove Moise's parity argument :: Estimated effort: 2+ weeks.
PLAN4_PHASE_D_LOG.md:69 advice Strategy C ‚Äî Reprove Moise's parity argument :: **Decision:** start with Strategy A in a fresh session. The bridge is one-time infrastructure that will also help `Theorem_GT_2_5/6` (already clean but fragile) and ¬ß4-onward planar topology.
PLAN4_PHASE_D_LOG.md:75 advice Strategy A ‚Äî concrete plan for next session :: 1. **Add a new section `‚ÄπReal¬≤ ‚Üî Complex bridge‚Ä∫` near the top of GeoTop.thy** (or, better, in `b/GeoTopBase.thy` so other files can use it).
PLAN4_PHASE_D_LOG.md:78 advice Strategy A ‚Äî concrete plan for next session :: 2. **Define and prove**: ```isabelle definition R2_to_C :: "real^2 \<Rightarrow> complex" where "R2_to_C v = Complex (v $ 1) (v $ 2)" definition C_to_R2 :: "complex \<Rightarrow> real^2" where "C_to_R2 z = vector [Re z, Im z]" ``` - `R2_to_C ‚àò C_to_R2 = id`, `C_to_R2 ‚àò R2_to_C = id` - `bounded_linear R2_to_C`, `bounded_linear C_to_R2` - `homeomorphism UNIV UNIV R2_to_C C_to_R2` - `norm (R2_to_C v) = norm v` (so spheres go to spheres)
PLAN4_PHASE_D_LOG.md:90 advice Strategy A ‚Äî concrete plan for next session :: 3. **Lift `simple_path`, `pathstart`, `pathfinish`, `path_image`** through the bridge: state and prove - `simple_path c \<longleftrightarrow> simple_path (R2_to_C \<circ> c)` (assuming `c :: real \<Rightarrow> real^2`) - `path_image (R2_to_C \<circ> c) = R2_to_C ` (path_image c)` - `pathstart (R2_to_C \<circ> c) = R2_to_C (pathstart c)`, etc.
PLAN4_PHASE_D_LOG.md:96 advice Strategy A ‚Äî concrete plan for next session :: 4. **State `Jordan_curve_real2`**: ```isabelle theorem Jordan_curve_real2: fixes c :: "real \<Rightarrow> real^2" assumes "simple_path c" and "pathfinish c = pathstart c" obtains inner outer where "inner \<noteq> {}" "open inner" "connected inner" "outer \<noteq> {}" "open outer" "connected outer" "bounded inner" "\<not> bounded outer" "inner \<inter> outer = {}" "inner \<union> outer = - path_image c" "frontier inner = path_image c" "frontier outer = path_image c" ``` Proof: apply `Jordan_curve [OF (simple_path (R2_to_C \<circ> c)) ...]`, get `inner_C outer_C`, take `inner = C_to_R2 ` inner_C`, `outer = C_to_R2 ` outer_C`.
PLAN4_PHASE_D_LOG.md:113 advice Strategy A ‚Äî concrete plan for next session :: 5. **From `geotop_is_polygon J`, derive a simple closed path `c`**: The `geotop_is_n_sphere` clause gives a homeomorphism `f: J \<leftrightarrow> S¬π`. Compose with the standard parametrisation of `S¬π` to get `c :: real \<Rightarrow> real^2` simple, closed, with `path_image c = J`.
PLAN4_PHASE_D_LOG.md:118 advice Strategy A ‚Äî concrete plan for next session :: 6. **Close `Lemma_GT_2_1a` and `Lemma_GT_2_1b`** by applying `Jordan_curve_real2` to the parametrisation, replacing the parity sketch.
PLAN4_PHASE_D_LOG.md:121 advice Strategy A ‚Äî concrete plan for next session :: 7. **Audit**: `thm_oracles Theorem_GT_2_1` should give empty list.

PLAN4_PHASE_D_LOG.md:903 advice Closed in this session :: The proof structure: 1. Hoist arc-distinctness facts to outer scope (B1‚âÝB2, B1‚âÝB3, B2‚âÝB3 from non-empty disjoint arc interiors). 2. For each component U: derive S = {Bi | Bi ‚äÜ frontier U} via h_clopen_in_int + h_full_arc. 3. Decomposition: frontier U ‚äÜ (‚ãÉS) ‚à™ E. 4. Cardinality of S: - card S ‚â§ 3 by `card_mono`. - card S ‚âÝ 3 by `h_at_most_two`. - card S ‚âÝ 1 by `h_at_least_two` (with translation to its awkward statement). - card S ‚âÝ 0 (the substantial proof ‚Äî see below). 5. So card S = 2; pick S = {i, j}; show frontier U = i ‚à™ j.
PLAN4_PHASE_D_LOG.md:916 advice Closed in this session :: The card S ‚âÝ 0 case (~70 lines): - If S = ‚àÖ: frontier U ‚äÜ E (‚â§ 2 points), hence finite. - `path_connected_complement_countable` (HOL-Analysis): R¬≤ minus countable set is path-connected (for DIM ‚â• 2), hence connected. - So `- frontier U` is connected. - `- frontier U` partitions into U and `UNIV - closure U` (both open, disjoint). - `connectedD` forces one to be empty. - U non-empty ‚üπ `UNIV - closure U = {}`, i.e., closure U = UNIV. - So M ‚äÜ frontier U ‚äÜ E (size ‚â§ 2), but M has uncountable interior arcs. Contradiction.

PLAN4_PHASE_D_LOG.md:1185 advice Closed in this session :: **`broken_line_interior_empty`** (~50 lines, oracle-empty): a broken line in `real^2` has empty topological interior. Proof via `invariance_of_dimension` (HOL-Analysis): if `interior B \<noteq> \<emptyset>`, take a point x in it, get the inverse homeomorphism g' : B \<to> [0,1], note g' continuous on the open `interior B` and injective on it. Apply `invariance_of_dimension` with f = g' : real^2 \<to> real to get DIM(real^2) \<le> DIM(real), i.e., 2 \<le> 1. Contradiction.

PLAN4_PHASE_D_LOG.md:1216 advice Closed in this session :: **`theta_graph_complement_has_two_components`** (~140 lines, oracle-empty): the complement \<open>UNIV - M\<close> of a polyhedral Œ∏-graph has \<open>\<ge> 2\<close> distinct components in `real^2`. Strategy: 1. Polygon \<open>B1 \<union> B2\<close> separates \<open>R^2\<close> into 2 components C1 and C2 (Theorem_GT_2_1). 2. Each Ci has a point not in M, because Ci is open non-empty and the only part of M outside \<open>B1 \<union> B2\<close> is \<open>B3\<close> which has empty 2D interior (`broken_line_interior_empty`). 3. Take points x1 ‚àà C1 - M, x2 ‚àà C2 - M. 4. The connected component D1 of \<open>R^2 - M\<close> through x1 is contained in C1 (via `connected_component_mono`), similar...

PLAN4_PHASE_D_LOG.md:1253 advice Findings :: We have at least 2 components D1, D2 of `R^2 - M`. Let U have frontier = M. Then closure U = U ‚à™ M. The other components V ‚âÝ U have closure V ‚äÜ R¬≤ - U (since V ‚à© U = ‚àÖ, V ‚à© M = ‚àÖ).
PLAN4_PHASE_D_LOG.md:1257 advice Findings :: By symmetry, frontier(R¬≤ - closure U) = M too. M is then covered by frontiers of components V ‚âÝ U.
PLAN4_PHASE_D_LOG.md:1260 advice Findings :: The key observation: at any point x ‚àà Int Bi (not an endpoint), the local 1-dim structure of Bi means at most 2 components of `R^2 - M` accumulate at x. If U is one of them and frontier U = M (so x ‚àà frontier U), at most one other component V ‚âÝ U has x in its frontier.
PLAN4_PHASE_D_LOG.md:1265 advice Findings :: For a Œ∏-graph M = B1 ‚à™ B2 ‚à™ B3 sharing only endpoints E, this gives a combinatorial constraint. Proof: - `polygon_components_card`: exactly 2 components. - `polygon_interior` and `polygon_exterior` are both components and distinct (one bounded, one unbounded). - So {polygon_interior, polygon_exterior} = the set of components. - C ‚àà this 2-element set; C ‚âÝ polygon_exterior (different boundedness). - Therefore C = polygon_interior.

Contradicts e being a non-degenerate closed segment.

PLAN4_PHASE_D_LOG.md:2730 advice Session 68 ‚Äî Two new oracle-empty helpers + sub-sorry application deferred :: - **\<open>affine_dim_1_distinct_meet_singleton\<close>**: Two distinct 1-dim affine subspaces in Euclidean space meet in \<le> 1 point. Proof: A \<inter> B is affine, aff_dim \<le> 1; if = 1, \<open>affine_dim_equal\<close> forces A = B; else aff_dim \<le> 0 means singleton or empty.
PLAN4_PHASE_D_LOG.md:2735 advice Session 68 ‚Äî Two new oracle-empty helpers + sub-sorry application deferred :: - **\<open>closed_segment_in_lines_aff_hull\<close>**: For e closed segment with aff_dim 1, \<open>e \<subseteq> \<Union>Ls\<close> (Ls finite distinct lines), then \<open>aff hull e \<in> Ls\<close>. Proof: contradiction; if aff hull e \<notin> Ls, each L \<in> Ls meets aff hull e in \<le> 1 point (using \<open>affine_dim_1_distinct_meet_singleton\<close>); so e \<subseteq> finitely many points; \<open>connected_finite_iff_sing\<close> + e has \<ge> 2 points = contradiction.

PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:3 advice Report: GeoTop / Moise Sections 3‚Äì4 formalization on `codex-dev34-cache` :: I examined the GitHub branch, the `tst` development layout, the project status reports, and the raw Isabelle theory fragments around the remaining proof holes. I did **not** run a local Isabelle build in this environment, so the conclusions below are based on the branch contents and the project‚Äôs own build/status artifacts rather than an independent kernel check.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:7 advice 1. Executive assessment :: The project is **not stuck because of missing syntax or random automation failures**. It is stuck at the natural hard point of this formalization: Moise‚Äôs Sections 3 and 4 repeatedly use planar-topology pictures as arguments, while Isabelle needs those pictures decomposed into reusable lemmas about finite complexes, polygonal disks, arcs, local charts, and graph carriers.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:9 advice 1. Executive assessment :: The current branch is very close structurally. The repository branch exists as `codex-dev34-cache`, and the `tst` directory contains a layered development around `dev34_pre`, `dev34_prefix`, `dev34_core`, and active `dev34` theories. The public branch page shows the repository and branch state, while the `tst` directory confirms the development is intentionally split into prefix, facts, graph facts, link facts, open-star facts, and active work layers. ([GitHub][1])
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:11 advice 1. Executive assessment :: The latest committed status file says the target stack still has **17 executable `sorry`s**: **12 in `tst/dev34_prefix`**, **0 in `tst/dev34_core`**, and **5 in active `tst/dev34`**. It also reports no remaining `sledgehammer` markers in the checked target set. The report‚Äôs completion criterion is clear: remove all `sorry`/`sledgehammer` from `tst/dev34_prefix`, `tst/dev34_core`, and `tst/dev34`, rebuild successfully, regenerate indexes, and commit. ([GitHub][2])
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:13 advice 1. Executive assessment :: The good news is that this is now a **finite, well-localized completion problem**, not an exploratory port. The bad news is that the remaining holes are exactly the places where Moise silently relies on nontrivial geometric or graph-theoretic facts. The worker should stop trying to ‚Äúfinish the theorem at the hole‚Äù with local proof search. The fastest route is to introduce a small number of sharply stated intermediate lemmas and then make the top-level theorems almost mechanical.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:15 advice 1. Executive assessment :: My strongest recommendation is this:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:17 advice 1. Executive assessment :: > Finish Sections 3 and 4 by proving five reusable lemma families: > **finite triangulated disk ear/free-triangle lemmas**, > **free-triangle fold/elimination lemmas with support control**, > **arc-crossing/separation lemmas inside a polygonal disk**, > **finite linear graph cycle/path classification lemmas**, and > **explicit local Euclidean chart lemmas for one-, two-, and three-triangle edge stars**.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:24 advice 1. Executive assessment :: Trying to close the remaining `sorry`s one by one without these abstractions will likely keep producing brittle proofs.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:26 advice 1. Executive assessment :: ---
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:30 advice 2. Current architecture and what it means :: The development is deliberately layered. The status file describes the active target stack as follows: `dev34_pre` is a dirty cached prefix, `dev34_prefix` holds much of the current Sections 3‚Äì4 formalization, `dev34_core` is reported clean, and active `dev34` contains the remaining top-level/local-chart work. PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:174 advice 5. Section 4.2: arc separation and disjointness :: Theorem 4.2 still has a hole named around `hD42_disjoint`. The raw snapshot shows the theorem statement and an unfinished disjointness/separation step. ([GitHub][4])
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:176 advice 5. Section 4.2: arc separation and disjointness :: This is likely Moise‚Äôs familiar argument: an arc in a disk separates certain boundary points, and if the wrong components meet, one can draw a crossing arc or broken line contradicting planar order.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:178 advice 5. Section 4.2: arc separation and disjointness :: The worker should resist proving this by set algebra alone. The right helper lemma is an **arc-crossing contradiction** inside a polygonal disk.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:180 advice 5. Section 4.2: arc separation and disjointness :: A good formal shape is:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:182 advice 5. Section 4.2: arc separation and disjointness :: ```isabelle polygon_disk_crossing_arc_contradiction: assumes "A is an arc in the polygonal disk I from P to R" and "B is an arc or broken line in I - A from Q to S" and "P Q R S occur in cyclic order on the boundary" shows False ```
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:190 advice 5. Section 4.2: arc separation and disjointness :: or equivalently:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:192 advice 5. Section 4.2: arc separation and disjointness :: ```isabelle arc_separates_opposite_boundary_points: assumes "A is an arc in I from P to R" and "P Q R S are cyclic on frontier I" shows "Q and S lie in different components of I - A" ```
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:199 advice 5. Section 4.2: arc separation and disjointness :: The proof should probably reuse whichever earlier theorem corresponds to Moise‚Äôs crossing/order result, rather than rebuilding Jordan separation from scratch. The older report says Section 4.2 and 4.4 are already framed among arc and component arguments, so this missing proof is probably intended to sit on top of earlier Sections 1‚Äì2 facts. ([GitHub][3])
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:201 advice 5. Section 4.2: arc separation and disjointness :: A practical completion strategy:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:203 advice 5. Section 4.2: arc separation and disjointness :: 1. Define the relevant components explicitly:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:205 advice 5. Section 4.2: arc separation and disjointness :: ```isabelle CQ = connected_component_set (I - A) Q' CS = connected_component_set (I - A) S' ```
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:210 advice 5. Section 4.2: arc separation and disjointness :: or whatever component notion the library uses.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:212 advice 5. Section 4.2: arc separation and disjointness :: 2. Prove `CQ ‚âÝ CS` by contradiction.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:214 advice 5. Section 4.2: arc separation and disjointness :: 3. From `CQ = CS`, extract a polygonal arc or broken line in `I - A` from a point near `Q` to a point near `S`.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:216 advice 5. Section 4.2: arc separation and disjointness :: 4. Attach short boundary-normal segments if necessary to make the endpoints exactly `Q` and `S`, while preserving disjointness from `A`.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:218 advice 5. Section 4.2: arc separation and disjointness :: 5. Apply the crossing-order theorem.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:220 advice 5. Section 4.2: arc separation and disjointness :: This is the kind of proof where most difficulty comes from endpoint hygiene. The worker should introduce endpoint-perturbation lemmas rather than repeatedly unfolding component definitions.

PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:238 advice 6. Section 4.4: bricks are the danger zone :: My recommendation is **not** to formalize arbitrary bricks from scratch. Instead, prove a regular-neighborhood theorem for compact polygonal arcs inside a triangulated disk.

PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:242 advice 6. PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:253 advice 6. Section 4.4: bricks are the danger zone :: Then the book‚Äôs brick decomposition can be represented as an implementation detail, not a theorem-level dependency. PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:265 advice 7. Figure 4.10 and graph realization :: Active `dev34` still has a core hole around `geotop_connected_nonisolated_finite_linear_graph_boundary_cycle_model_dev34`, and a wrapper immediately calls it. This is the Figure 4.10 cyclic graph realization problem. ([GitHub][5])
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:267 advice 7. Figure 4.10 and graph realization :: This should be treated as a **finite graph classification problem first**, and as a geometric realization problem second.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:269 advice 7. Figure 4.10 and graph realization :: The tempting but bad approach is to reason directly about a carrier embedded in the plane and try to construct the boundary subdivision geometrically in one proof. The better approach is:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:271 advice 7. Figure 4.10 and graph realization :: 1. Define the abstract graph of vertices and edges of the finite 1-complex.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:273 advice 7. Figure 4.10 and graph realization :: 2. Prove a combinatorial classification:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:275 advice 7. Figure 4.10 and graph realization :: * finite, * connected, * nonisolated, * linear graph, * every vertex has degree 2 implies the graph is a simple cycle.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:282 advice 7. Figure 4.10 and graph realization :: 3. Produce a cyclic list of vertices and edges.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:284 advice 7. Figure 4.10 and graph realization :: 4. Separately prove that every finite simple cycle graph is PL-isomorphic to a subdivision of a triangle boundary, square boundary, or polygonal circle.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:286 advice 7. Figure 4.10 and graph realization :: 5. Use the existing geometric carrier facts only to show the hypotheses of the combinatorial lemma.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:288 advice 7. Figure 4.10 and graph realization :: This also applies to the broken-line fan/boundary-vertex material. The raw active file shows recent proof structure around a finite linear graph broken-line/fan model, suggesting the development is already moving in this direction. ([GitHub][5])
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:290 advice 7. Figure 4.10 and graph realization :: The worker should make this split explicit:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:292 advice 7. Figure 4.10 and graph realization :: ```isabelle finite_connected_degree2_graph_has_cycle_listing cycle_listing_realizes_boundary_subdivision linear_graph_polygon_carrier_has_degree2_vertices ```
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:298 advice 7. Figure 4.10 and graph realization :: Then Figure 4.10 becomes mostly composition.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:300 advice 7. Figure 4.10 and graph realization :: The main pitfall is degree bookkeeping at endpoints. If the carrier is a circle-like graph, every vertex has degree 2. If it is a broken-line arc, exactly two vertices have degree 1 and the rest have degree 2. Those two cases should be proved separately, not interleaved.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:302 advice 7. Figure 4.10 and graph realization :: ---
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:306 advice 8. Local chart obligations for Theorems 4.8‚Äì4.10 :: The active `dev34` file still contains local Euclidean holes. These are not global topology problems; they should be solved by explicit normal forms.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:310 advice 8.1 One-sided edge: semicircle separation :: The raw active file shows a remaining `sorry` around a lemma named like `geotop_edge_one_side_simplex_local_semicircle_radius_separates_domain_dev34`, and the nearby wrapper reduces the one-sided local chart argument to this explicit semicircle-radius separation lemma. ([GitHub][5])
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:312 advice 8.1 One-sided edge: semicircle separation :: That is good. The correct proof should be purely Euclidean.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:314 advice 8.1 One-sided edge: semicircle separation :: Normalize the configuration by an affine homeomorphism:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:316 advice 8.1 One-sided edge: semicircle separation :: * the edge becomes a diameter segment on the x-axis, * the unique incident triangle lies in the upper half-plane, * the point of interest is at the origin or another simple coordinate, * the chosen radius is small enough that the local star intersects the circle/semicircle in the standard way.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:321 advice 8.1 One-sided edge: semicircle separation :: Then prove separation using a continuous coordinate function, not topological guesswork. For example, the complement of a small semicircle in a half-disk has two relevant local sides. One can show two chosen points cannot be connected without crossing the semicircle by composing any hypothetical path with a radial/angle coordinate or by using a line/circle crossing lemma already present.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:323 advice 8.1 One-sided edge: semicircle separation :: The important instruction to the worker is: **specialize the lemma to the exact normalized semicircle used by the proof.** A more general theorem about arbitrary semicircles in arbitrary domains will be slower.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:327 advice 8.2 Three incident triangles: small-circle complement connectedness :: Another active hole is `geotop_three_incident_small_circle_complement_connected_explicit_dev34`. The status report also identifies this as one of the best next active targets. The raw file shows the explicit lemma still has `sorry`, and nearby code constructs a local finite carrier, chooses an epsilon, and invokes that explicit complement-connected lemma. ([GitHub][2])
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:329 advice 8.2 Three incident triangles: small-circle complement connectedness :: This is the dual of the one-sided edge case. In the one-sided case, the local semicircle separates the local domain. In the three-incident case, the extra sector gives a path around the obstruction, so the punctured/complemented local domain remains connected.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:331 advice 8.2 Three incident triangles: small-circle complement connectedness :: Again, use a normal form. Put the edge on the x-axis and let the incident 2-simplexes occupy three sectors around it. Choose a small circle centered at the point. Then show any two points in the local carrier minus the circle can be connected by polygonal paths using the third sector as the bypass.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:333 advice 8.2 Three incident triangles: small-circle complement connectedness :: A strong but manageable lemma is:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:335 advice 8.2 Three incident triangles: small-circle complement connectedness :: ```isabelle three_sector_disk_minus_small_circle_path_connected: assumes "U contains three sectors around p" and "0 < eps" and "closed_ball p eps ‚äÜ local model" shows "path_connected (U - sphere p eps)" ```
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:342 advice 8.2 Three incident triangles: small-circle complement connectedness :: Then derive the project‚Äôs `top1_connected_on` conclusion from path-connectedness.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:344 advice 8.2 Three incident triangles: small-circle complement connectedness :: Do not try to prove connectedness directly from arbitrary open-set definitions. Construct paths.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:348 advice 8.3 Local Jordan side containment :: The active file also has a remaining `sorry` in `geotop_2cell_chart_1sphere_complement_not_connected_dev34`, around showing that the inner and outer sides of a charted 1-sphere have the required containment/intersection properties. ([GitHub][5])
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:350 advice 8.3 Local Jordan side containment :: This is another case where the generic statement may be too hard. The proof should be specialized to the actual small circle or 1-sphere chosen in the local chart.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:352 advice 8.3 Local Jordan side containment :: The right helper lemma is:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:354 advice 8.3 Local Jordan side containment :: ```isabelle small_circle_in_chart_has_inner_side_inside_chart_domain: assumes "closed_ball p r ‚äÜ chart_domain" and "J = sphere p r" shows "bounded_component (UNIV - J) ‚äÜ chart_domain" ```
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:361 advice 8.3 Local Jordan side containment :: and:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:363 advice 8.3 Local Jordan side containment :: ```isabelle small_circle_in_chart_has_outer_side_meeting_chart_domain: assumes "chart_domain is a neighborhood of p" and "J = sphere p r" shows "unbounded_component (UNIV - J) ‚à© chart_domain ‚âÝ {}" ```
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:370 advice 8.3 Local Jordan side containment :: The first is just ‚Äúthe disk bounded by the small circle lies inside the selected local ball.‚Äù The second is usually witnessed by a point in the chart domain outside the small circle, chosen along a ray or inside the local star.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert.md:372 advice 8.3 Local Jordan side containment :: This will be much easier than proving a fully abstract Jordan-side statement for arbitrary embedded 1-spheres.

PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:175 advice 4.1 Finish the selected boundary-ear lemma first :: The highest-priority Section 3 target is:

PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:181 advice 4.1 Finish the selected boundary-ear lemma first :: Do not prove this by drawing the triangulated disk inside the theorem. It should be a finite combinatorial lemma about selected boundary edges in a triangulated polygonal disk.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:183 advice 4.1 Finish the selected boundary-ear lemma first :: The current statement appears to need two distinct 2-simplexes, each carrying a selected boundary 1-face. The wrapper then obtains the ordinary two-boundary-triangle conclusion. ([GitHub][3])
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:185 advice 4.1 Finish the selected boundary-ear lemma first :: The most direct proof route seems to be:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:187 advice 4.1 Finish the selected boundary-ear lemma first :: 1. Define the set of selected boundary edges globally:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:189 advice 4.1 Finish the selected boundary-ear lemma first :: ```isabelle B = {e ‚àà K. geotop_simplex_dim e 1 ‚àß e ‚äÜ frontier I ‚àß selected e} ```
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:193 advice 4.1 Finish the selected boundary-ear lemma first :: 2. Prove that this set has at least three elements, or at least enough elements that it cannot be owned by a single 2-simplex.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:195 advice 4.1 Finish the selected boundary-ear lemma first :: 3. Prove every selected boundary edge is contained in at least one 2-simplex of the disk triangulation.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:197 advice 4.1 Finish the selected boundary-ear lemma first :: 4. Prove a 2-simplex can own at most three selected boundary edges.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:199 advice 4.1 Finish the selected boundary-ear lemma first :: 5. Use the already-proved all-boundary obstruction: if a single 2-simplex owned all three of its edges as boundary edges, then the disk would be forced into that triangle, contradicting the assumption that there are more than two 2-simplexes. The current prefix file already has this all-boundary closure/no-other-simplex infrastructure. ([GitHub][3])
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:201 advice 4.1 Finish the selected boundary-ear lemma first :: 6. Conclude that selected boundary edges must be attached to at least two distinct 2-simplexes.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:203 advice 4.1 Finish the selected boundary-ear lemma first :: This approach is better aligned with the existing code than a full dual-graph proof. A dual-graph proof is tempting, but triangulation dual graphs of disks can have cycles, and the formal overhead can grow quickly. The current project already has the all-boundary closure machinery; exploit it.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:205 advice 4.1 Finish the selected boundary-ear lemma first :: A useful intermediate lemma would be:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:207 advice 4.1 Finish the selected boundary-ear lemma first :: ```isabelle polygon_disk_selected_boundary_edges_not_all_on_one_2simplex: assumes "polygon_disk_triangulation K I" and "card {s ‚àà K. geotop_simplex_dim s 2} > 2" and "‚ãÄe. e ‚àà selected_boundary_edges K I ‚üπ e ‚äÜ t" and "geotop_simplex_dim t 2" shows False ```
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:216 advice 4.1 Finish the selected boundary-ear lemma first :: Then the target lemma becomes a cardinality/choice argument.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:220 advice 4.2 Then close Theorem 3.3 as a consequence :: Once the selected-ear lemma is proved, avoid adding more geometry to Theorem 3.3. The raw file already suggests that Theorem 3.3 has been converted into a strong free-count/decomposition wrapper. ([GitHub][3])
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:222 advice 4.2 Then close Theorem 3.3 as a consequence :: The final Theorem 3.3 proof should look like:

PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:249 advice 4.3 Theorem 3.4: make the fold step a named lemma :: Split the fold lemma into cases:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:251 advice 4.3 Theorem 3.4: make the fold step a named lemma :: **Case 1: one boundary edge.** This is the standard ‚Äúremove an ear‚Äù case. The local fold is supported in the triangle plus its adjacent simplex. The reduced complex has one fewer 2-simplex.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:254 advice 4.3 Theorem 3.4: make the fold step a named lemma :: **Case 2: two boundary edges.** This is often geometrically easier but set-theoretically annoying. The free triangle is a corner of the disk; removing it changes the boundary polygon by replacing two edges with the remaining edge.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:257 advice 4.3 Theorem 3.4: make the fold step a named lemma :: **Case 3: three boundary edges.** This should be impossible in the large case or should collapse to the already-proved all-boundary closure lemma.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:260 advice 4.3 Theorem 3.4: make the fold step a named lemma :: **Case 4: no boundary edge.** This should contradict the ‚Äúfree boundary triangle‚Äù assumptions.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:263 advice 4.3 Theorem 3.4: make the fold step a named lemma :: The worker should avoid trying to make one overloaded proof handle all cases without named case lemmas. The case split is a feature, not a problem.

PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:305 advice 5. Section 4.2: finish the arc-separation lemma, not the theorem body :: This is a classic Moise picture: an arc from `P` to `R` in a disk separates the boundary points/arcs lying on opposite sides in cyclic order `P Q R S`.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:307 advice 5. Section 4.2: finish the arc-separation lemma, not the theorem body :: The worker should prove a reusable lemma:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:309 advice 5. PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:319 advice 5. Section 4.2: finish the arc-separation lemma, not the theorem body :: or, if the theorem uses small neighborhoods around `Q` and `S`, formulate it directly for those component sets.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:321 advice 5. Section 4.2: finish the arc-separation lemma, not the theorem body :: The proof should be by contradiction:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:323 advice 5. Section 4.2: finish the arc-separation lemma, not the theorem body :: 1. assume `Q` and `S` lie in the same component of `I - A`; 2. extract a polygonal arc or broken line from `Q` to `S` avoiding `A`; 3. combine this with the boundary cyclic-order data; 4. apply the earlier crossing theorem / Jordan arc theorem already formalized in Sections 1‚Äì2.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:328 advice 5. Section 4.2: finish the arc-separation lemma, not the theorem body :: Do not prove `hD42_disjoint` by component algebra alone. The disjointness is not algebraic; it is the separation theorem. The theorem body should only instantiate the reusable lemma.

PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:473 advice 8.1 One-sided semicircle separation :: The open lemma is intended to choose a radius `r` and an arc `A` in the unique incident 2-simplex so that `U - A` is not connected. ([GitHub][4])
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:475 advice 8.1 One-sided semicircle separation :: The intended proof should be Euclidean and explicit:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:477 advice 8.1 One-sided semicircle separation :: 1. Affinely normalize the edge to the x-axis and the unique incident triangle to a closed half-plane sector. 2. Choose `r < s` small enough that:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:480 advice 8.1 One-sided semicircle separation :: ```isabelle sphere p r ‚à© local_carrier = sphere p r ‚à© œÉ ``` 3. Let:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:485 advice 8.1 One-sided semicircle separation :: ```isabelle A = sphere p r ‚à© œÉ ```
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:489 advice 8.1 One-sided semicircle separation :: which is a semicircular crosscut of the local half-disk. 4. Choose two witness points on opposite sides of `A`. 5. Show any path between them would make the continuous function `x ‚Ü¶ dist x p` pass from `< r` to `> r`; hence it hits `sphere p r ‚à© œÉ = A`. 6. Therefore the two witnesses lie in different components of `U - A`.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:494 advice 8.1 One-sided semicircle separation :: The proof should use the norm/intermediate-value argument, not a general Jordan theorem. This is one of the rare places where an analytic coordinate proof is simpler than abstract topology.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:496 advice 8.1 One-sided semicircle separation :: However, the worker should carefully re-check the exact statement. If `U` is an arbitrary subset of the polyhedron that merely contains a small local ball, then `U - A` might reconnect around the outside unless additional hypotheses prevent that. The lemma is safe if `U` is the relevant local disk/chart domain, or if the conclusion is only about a local domain such as `U ‚à© ball p s`. If the current statement lacks that upper-locality or connectedness information, strengthen the assumptions or weaken the conclusion to the exact local contradiction needed.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:500 advice 8.2 Three incident triangles and small-circle complement connectedness :: The open three-incident lemma tries to show that removing a small circle does **not** disconnect the relevant domain in the three-incident-edge case. The surrounding code has already built much of the finite cover/radius setup and invokes the explicit connectedness lemma. ([GitHub][4])
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:502 advice 8.2 Three incident triangles and small-circle complement connectedness :: Again, first verify the statement. A global conclusion like:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:504 advice 8.2 Three incident triangles and small-circle complement connectedness :: ```isabelle top1_connected_on (U - sphere p eps) ```
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:508 advice 8.2 Three incident triangles and small-circle complement connectedness :: usually requires some connectedness/locality hypothesis on `U`. A completely arbitrary `U` containing the small local carrier could have unrelated disconnected parts. If the visible assumptions do not imply `top1_connected_on U` or a local chart-domain condition, the lemma may be over-strong.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:510 advice 8.2 Three incident triangles and small-circle complement connectedness :: There is also a possible shortcut worth checking before investing heavily in this proof. Recent project reports mention same-complex two-triangle opposite-side/local-model infrastructure, and the active file now has strong local fan/link facts. ([GitHub][5])
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:512 advice 8.2 Three incident triangles and small-circle complement connectedness :: In an embedded planar complex, three distinct 2-simplexes sharing the same edge are often impossible already by elementary affine geometry: two triangles sharing an edge must lie on opposite sides or violate the complex-intersection condition; a third triangle has no remaining side available. If the project has already proved a lemma of that form, then the entire ‚Äúthree incident edge faces‚Äù case can potentially be closed by contradiction before reaching the small-circle connectedness proof.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:514 advice 8.2 Three incident triangles and small-circle complement connectedness :: The worker should check whether a lemma like this already exists:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:516 advice 8.2 Three incident triangles and small-circle complement connectedness :: ```isabelle same_complex_two_2simplex_shared_edge_opposite_sides ```
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:520 advice 8.2 Three incident triangles and small-circle complement connectedness :: If yes, prove:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:522 advice 8.2 Three incident triangles and small-circle complement connectedness :: ```isabelle no_three_2simplexes_share_edge_in_planar_complex ```
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:526 advice 8.2 Three incident triangles and small-circle complement connectedness :: Then use that to eliminate the three-incident case. This may be dramatically simpler than formalizing Moise‚Äôs topological nonseparation picture.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:528 advice 8.2 Three incident triangles and small-circle complement connectedness :: If book-faithfulness requires preserving Moise‚Äôs route, then keep the three-incident small-circle lemma, but make it local and constructive:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:530 advice 8.2 Three incident triangles and small-circle complement connectedness :: ```isabelle three_page_local_star_minus_small_circle_connected: assumes "local star near p is exactly the three incident sectors" and "U_local = ball p s ‚à© polyhedron K" and "sphere p eps ‚äÜ U_local" and "0 < eps" "eps < s" shows "top1_connected_on (U_local - sphere p eps)" ```
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:539 advice 8.2 Three incident triangles and small-circle complement connectedness :: Then transfer this to the theorem‚Äôs actual `U` only after proving the needed connectedness and inclusion hypotheses.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:543 advice 8.3 Chart/Jordan-side containment :: The remaining chart-side step asks for inner/outer components of the chart image of a 1-sphere, with the bounded side contained in the chart image of `U` and the outer side meeting the chart image of `U`. ([GitHub][4])
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:545 advice 8.3 Chart/Jordan-side containment :: Do not prove this for arbitrary embedded 1-spheres in arbitrary open sets. Prove the exact convex-container lemma needed after mapping the local 2-cell to a standard triangle.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:547 advice 8.3 Chart/Jordan-side containment :: A good lemma is:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:549 advice 8.3 Chart/Jordan-side containment :: ```isabelle jordan_curve_inside_open_convex_set_bounded_side_subset: assumes "convex C" and "open C" and "J ‚äÜ C" and "geotop_is_1sphere J" and "bounded_component B of UNIV - J" shows "B ‚äÜ C" ```
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:559 advice 8.3 Chart/Jordan-side containment :: For the standard triangle, `C` is the interior of the 2-simplex. Since `C` is convex, any point outside `C` can be connected to infinity by a ray or polygonal path avoiding `J`, so it belongs to the outer component. Therefore the bounded component must be inside `C`.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:561 advice 8.3 Chart/Jordan-side containment :: Then prove that the outer component meets `C` by choosing a point of `C` outside the bounded side, for instance near the triangle boundary but still in the interior.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:563 advice 8.3 Chart/Jordan-side containment :: This should close `hchart_image_sep` without developing a broad Jordan-side theory.

PLAN_zero_sorry-expert1.md:640 advice 10. Main recommendations in compact form :: The most important actions are:

PLAN_zero_sorry-expert2.md:153 advice 4.1 Branch vertex local cutpoint package :: The branch-vertex lemma says that if a finite linear graph has a simple-closed-curve carrier and a vertex with more than two incident edges, then deleting that vertex disconnects the carrier. It is used to show that a polygonal finite linear graph cannot have a branch vertex. ([GitHub][5])
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert2.md:155 advice 4.1 Branch vertex local cutpoint package :: Be careful here: the statement relies crucially on the **simple-closed-curve carrier** hypothesis. In an arbitrary finite graph, a vertex of degree greater than two does **not** necessarily imply that deleting the vertex disconnects the graph; theta-like graphs can reconnect away from the vertex. So the proof should not silently generalize to ‚Äúdegree > 2 implies cutpoint‚Äù for all finite graphs.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert2.md:157 advice 4.1 Branch vertex local cutpoint package :: Recommended proof route:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert2.md:159 advice 4.1 Branch vertex local cutpoint package :: 1. Use the simple-closed-curve/local-1-manifold property of the carrier near `w`. 2. Show a sufficiently small local neighborhood of `w` in the finite linear graph consists of one open germ for each incident edge. 3. More than two incident edges gives at least three local punctured germs near `w`. 4. A simple closed curve minus a point has only the two local sides allowed by a 1-manifold circle model. 5. Contradict the ‚Äúmore than two incident edges‚Äù local structure.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert2.md:165 advice 4.1 Branch vertex local cutpoint package :: A more combinatorial alternative is to prove that if `polyhedron L - {w}` were connected, then at least three incident germs plus connecting arcs away from `w` would create a theta subgraph in a simple closed curve, contradicting the existing simple-closed-curve facts. That is likely harder than the local-degree argument unless the theta machinery is already very easy to instantiate.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert2.md:169 advice 4.2 Cycle orbit no non-adjacent reverse :: The `horbit_no_nonadjacent_reversed_book` hole should be handled as a pure finite-cycle enumeration lemma, not a planar geometry lemma. The current proof is already using an oriented successor orbit. The missing statement appears to rule out the same unoriented edge appearing later with reversed orientation except at the intended adjacent/cyclic positions. ([GitHub][6])
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert2.md:171 advice 4.2 Cycle orbit no non-adjacent reverse :: Recommended lemma shape:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert2.md:173 advice 4.2 Cycle orbit no non-adjacent reverse :: ```isabelle oriented_successor_orbit_no_nonadjacent_reverse: assumes "degree_two finite connected linear graph data" and "orbit is minimal periodic" and "i < n" "j < n" and "edge_at i = reverse_edge_at j" shows "i = j + 1 mod n ‚à® j = i + 1 mod n" ```
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert2.md:182 advice 4.2 Cycle orbit no non-adjacent reverse :: The key idea is minimality/injectivity of the oriented edge-state orbit before its first return. If a non-adjacent reversed edge appears, then the successor map has revisited an oriented state or its forced predecessor too early, contradicting the minimal orbit period.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert2.md:184 advice 4.2 Cycle orbit no non-adjacent reverse :: Do not reason about geometric carriers here. This is a graph-state/permutation proof.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert2.md:188 advice 4.3 Two cut path carriers meet only at `P,Q` :: The `hpoly_inter_subset_book` hole should then become straightforward. The proof should take an arbitrary point
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert2.md:190 advice 4.3 Two cut path carriers meet only at `P,Q` :: ```isabelle x ‚àà polyhedron K1 ‚à© polyhedron K2 ```
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert2.md:194 advice 4.3 Two cut path carriers meet only at `P,Q` :: and reduce by simplex membership:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert2.md:196 advice 4.3 Two cut path carriers meet only at `P,Q` :: 1. Pick simplexes `œÉ1 ‚àà K1` and `œÉ2 ‚àà K2` with `x ‚àà œÉ1 ‚à© œÉ2`. 2. Since this is a finite linear complex, the intersection of two 1-dimensional simplexes is either empty, a vertex, or the same edge. 3. If the intersection contains an edge, then the same edge occurs in both cut paths. The no-nonadjacent-reverse lemma rules this out except for the cut boundary edges. 4. If the intersection is a vertex, use cyclic-orbit vertex uniqueness: a vertex appears in both subpaths only if it is one of the cut vertices `P` or `Q`.
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert2.md:201 advice 4.3 Two cut path carriers meet only at `P,Q` :: The existing proof already has a helper around ‚Äúcycle cut if boundary edges absent and poly intersection subset,‚Äù so the aim should be to prove precisely the missing `polyhedron K1 ‚à© polyhedron K2 ‚äÜ {P,Q}` fact and then let the existing downstream infrastructure fire. ([GitHub][6])
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert2.md:205 advice 4.4 Active Figure 4.10 boundary cycle realization :: After the graph split closes, move immediately to:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert2.md:207 advice 4.4 Active Figure 4.10 boundary cycle realization :: ```isabelle geotop_finite_connected_degree_two_linear_graph_boundary_subdivision_model_dev34 ```
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert2.md:211 advice 4.4 Active Figure 4.10 boundary cycle realization :: The active file‚Äôs own comment gives the right proof outline: enumerate the oriented successor orbit, construct a subdivision of the frontier of a standard 2-simplex with matching cyclic length, and define the isomorphism by the cyclic vertex list. ([GitHub][8])
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert2.md:213 advice 4.4 Active Figure 4.10 boundary cycle realization :: The crucial implementation advice is to build two lemmas:
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert2.md:215 advice 4.4 Active Figure 4.10 boundary cycle realization :: ```isabelle finite_degree_two_graph_has_cyclic_vertex_listing cyclic_listing_realizes_standard_triangle_boundary_subdivision ```
PLAN_zero_sorry-expert2.md:220 advice 4.4 Active Figure 4.10 boundary cycle realization :: The first lemma should know nothing about the standard 2-simplex. The second should know nothing about the original graph except the cyclic list. This split will prevent the proof from mixing graph connectivity, orbit minimality, affine geometry, and complex-isomorphism bookkeeping in one place.
