REPORT_260425.md:597 advice Appendix C ‚Äî Numerical sorry-trajectory table :: Total post-Introduction sorries (in `GeoTop.thy`): **76**. Total in the foundational `GeoTopBase0`: **1** (the buggy lemma). Total in the active `GeoTopBase`: **0**.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:5 advice What we are formalizing :: We are formalizing parts of E. E. Moise, *Geometric Topology in Dimensions 2 and 3* (Springer GTM 47, 1977), in Isabelle/HOL. Moise's Theorem 1 (page 5 of Introduction) states:
REPORT_LITERAL.md:7 advice What we are formalizing :: > **Theorem 1 (Moise).** Every two subdivisions of the same complex have a common subdivision.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:9 advice What we are formalizing :: Moise does not prove this in the text; it is treated as background, with the proof presumably from a standard algebraic topology reference (Munkres' *Elements of Algebraic Topology* is the most common).
REPORT_LITERAL.md:11 advice What we are formalizing :: Our formalization follows Moise's approach: prove Theorem 1 via the auxiliary fact that some iterated barycentric subdivision of K refines any given subdivision of K. Specifically:
REPORT_LITERAL.md:17 advice Statement :: Let `K, K'` be (Euclidean) simplicial complexes in ‚Ñù‚Åø. Assume `K` is finite, and `K'` is a subdivision of `K` (in Munkres' sense ‚Äî see definitions below). Define the iterated barycentric subdivision `Sd·µê K` recursively: `Sd‚Å∞ K = K`, `Sd·µê‚Å∫¬π K = Sd(Sd·µê K)`.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:19 advice Statement :: **Claim (the literal refinement theorem):** There exists `m ‚àà ‚Ñï` such that `Sd·µê K` is a subdivision of `K'` (in Munkres' sense).
REPORT_LITERAL.md:22 advice Statement :: Equivalently, by Munkres' Definition 15.1.1:
REPORT_LITERAL.md:24 advice Statement :: > ‚àÉ m. ‚àÄœÑ ‚àà Sd·µê K. ‚àÉ œÉ' ‚àà K'. œÑ ‚äÜ œÉ' (LITERAL set-containment)
REPORT_LITERAL.md:28 advice Definitions used :: (Following Munkres, *Elements of Algebraic Topology*, ¬ß15.)
REPORT_LITERAL.md:30 advice Definitions used :: - A **(Euclidean) complex** `K` is a collection of simplices in ‚Ñù‚Åø such that: (K.0) Each `œÉ ‚àà K` is a (closed) Euclidean simplex. (K.1) Every face of every `œÉ ‚àà K` is in `K`. (K.2) For every `œÉ, œÑ ‚àà K`, if `œÉ ‚à© œÑ ‚âÝ ‚àÖ` then `œÉ ‚à© œÑ` is a face of each. (K.3) Every `œÉ ‚àà K` has an open neighborhood meeting only finitely many simplices of `K`.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:36 advice Definitions used :: - The **polyhedron** `|K|` is `‚ãÉ K`, with the subspace topology from ‚Ñù‚Åø.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:38 advice Definitions used :: - A complex `K'` is a **subdivision** of `K` if: (S.1) Each simplex of `K'` is contained in a simplex of `K` (literal set-containment). (S.2) Each simplex of `K` equals the union of finitely many simplices of `K'`.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:42 advice Definitions used :: - The **first barycentric subdivision** `Sd K` is constructed by recursively starring the `p`-skeleton from the barycenters of `(p+1)`-simplices (Munkres Definition, p. 85). Equivalently (Munkres Lemma 15.3, p. 85), `Sd K` consists of all simplices of the form `[œÉÃÇ‚ÇÅ œÉÃÇ‚ÇÇ ... œÉÃÇ‚Çô]` where `œÉ‚ÇÅ > œÉ‚ÇÇ > ... > œÉ‚Çô` is a strictly-descending chain of simplices of `K` and `œÉÃÇ` denotes the barycenter of `œÉ`.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:44 advice Definitions used :: - The **mesh** `‚ÄñK‚Äñ` is the supremum of diameters of simplices in `K`.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:48 advice What is in Munkres ¬ß15-17 :: Munkres provides:
REPORT_LITERAL.md:50 advice What is in Munkres ¬ß15-17 :: 1. **Theorem 15.4 (Mesh shrinkage):** Given a finite complex `K`, a metric for `|K|`, and `Œµ > 0`, there is `N` such that each simplex of `Sd·¥∫ K` has diameter less than `Œµ`. Specifically `‚ÄñSd·¥∫ K‚Äñ ‚â§ (n/(n+1))·¥∫ ¬∑ ‚ÄñK‚Äñ` where `n = dim K`.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:52 advice What is in Munkres ¬ß15-17 :: 2. **Theorem 16.1 (Finite Simplicial Approximation Theorem):** Let `K, L` be complexes with `K` finite. Given a continuous map `h : |K| ‚Üí |L|`, there is `N` such that `h` has a simplicial approximation `f : Sd·¥∫ K ‚Üí L`.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:54 advice What is in Munkres ¬ß15-17 :: Here `f : Sd·¥∫ K ‚Üí L` is a SIMPLICIAL MAP ‚Äî it sends `Sd·¥∫ K`-vertices to `L`-vertices, and each simplex `œÑ ‚àà Sd·¥∫ K` is mapped to a simplex `f(œÑ) ‚àà L`.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:56 advice What is in Munkres ¬ß15-17 :: 3. **Lemma 17.1:** If `K'` is a subdivision of `K`, then the identity `i : |K| ‚Üí |K|` has a simplicial approximation `g : K' ‚Üí K`.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:60 advice Why these are insufficient for our claim :: The literal refinement claim says **œÑ ‚äÜ œÉ'** as sets in ‚Ñù‚Åø.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:62 advice Why these are insufficient for our claim :: The simplicial approximation theorem says **`f(œÑ) ‚äÜ L`** (image is a simplex).
REPORT_LITERAL.md:64 advice Why these are insufficient for our claim :: These are different statements:
REPORT_LITERAL.md:66 advice Why these are insufficient for our claim :: - For `h = id : |K| ‚Üí |K'|` (when `K'` is a subdivision of `K`, so `|K| = |K'|`), Theorem 16.1 gives `f : Sd·¥∫ K ‚Üí K'` simplicial, with the **star condition**: `St(v, Sd·¥∫ K) ‚äÜ St(f(v), K')` for each vertex `v` of `Sd·¥∫ K`.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:68 advice Why these are insufficient for our claim :: - For `œÑ ‚àà Sd·¥∫ K` with vertices `V_œÑ`, the simplicial map gives `f(V_œÑ) ‚äÜ V(œÉ')` for some `œÉ' ‚àà K'`. Each `x ‚àà œÑ` lies in `‚ãÇ_{v ‚àà V_œÑ} St(f(v), K')`. By Munkres' Lemma 14.4, this intersection is non-empty iff `f(V_œÑ)` spans a `K'`-simplex (which it does, since `f` is simplicial).
REPORT_LITERAL.md:70 advice Why these are insufficient for our claim :: - But this places each `x` in **some** `K'`-simplex `œÑ'_x` containing `œÉ'` as a face. The simplex `œÑ'_x` may differ for different `x ‚àà œÑ`.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:72 advice Why these are insufficient for our claim :: In particular, **œÑ ‚äÜ œÑ'** for a single fixed `œÑ' ‚àà K'` is **not** a direct consequence of the simplicial approximation theorem.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:76 advice Counterexample to a simpler formulation we tried :: Define for `œÉ' ‚àà K'` with `œÉ' ‚äÜ œÉ_K` (some `K`-simplex) and `x ‚àà rel-interior œÉ'`: > `Œ¥(x, œÉ', œÉ_K) = dist(x, œÉ_K \ œÉ')` or `dist(x, œÉ_K \ rel-interior œÉ')`.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:79 advice Counterexample to a simpler formulation we tried :: We hoped that for `œÑ ‚äÜ œÉ_K` with `x ‚àà œÑ ‚à© rel-interior œÉ'` and `diam œÑ < Œ¥`, we'd have `œÑ ‚äÜ œÉ'`.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:81 advice Counterexample to a simpler formulation we tried :: **Counterexample:** Take `œÉ_K` = closed triangle `[a, b, c]`, `œÉ'` = closed edge `[a, b]` (a face of `œÉ_K`). Take `x = midpoint of edge ab ‚àà rel-interior œÉ'`. Points in `œÉ_K \ œÉ'` (the triangle interior) can be made arbitrarily close to `x` by approaching `x` from inside the triangle. So `dist(x, œÉ_K \ œÉ') = 0`. The same holds for `dist(x, œÉ_K \ rel-interior œÉ')`.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:83 advice Counterexample to a simpler formulation we tried :: So a per-point rel-distance bound on `(x, œÉ', œÉ_K)` does **not** give a positive lower bound, even when `œÉ'` is a face of `œÉ_K`.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:87 advice Counterexample to a "K'-carrier nesting" formulation we tried :: For `œÑ ‚àà Sd K` (the first barycentric subdivision) with `Sd`-vertices `V_œÑ = barycenter(set c)` for some `K`-flag `c = [œÉ‚ÇÄ ‚ää œÉ‚ÇÅ ‚ää ... ‚ää œÉ‚Çö]`, the `K`-carriers of `V_œÑ` are exactly the chain elements `{œÉ‚ÇÄ, ..., œÉ‚Çö}`.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:89 advice Counterexample to a "K'-carrier nesting" formulation we tried :: We hoped that for `K'` a subdivision of `K` and `K'-carrier` of each `bary(œÉ·µ¢)` defined as the unique `K'`-simplex `œÉ'·µ¢` with `bary(œÉ·µ¢) ‚àà rel-interior œÉ'·µ¢`, the `œÉ'·µ¢` would be **nested** (`œÉ'‚ÇÄ ‚äÜ œÉ'‚ÇÅ ‚äÜ ... ‚äÜ œÉ'‚Çö`), so taking `œÉ'‚Çö` would give `V_œÑ ‚äÜ œÉ'‚Çö`.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:91 advice Counterexample to a "K'-carrier nesting" formulation we tried :: **Counterexample:** Take `K` = closed segment `[a, b]`. `K`-flag `c = [{a}, [a, b]]`. `Sd K`-vertices = `{a, midpoint(ab)}`. Take `K'` to be a subdivision of `[a, b]` into many sub-segments: `[a, p‚ÇÅ], [p‚ÇÅ, p‚ÇÇ], ..., [p‚Çô, b]` where `p_{n/2} = midpoint(ab)`. Then: - `K'-carrier(a) = {a}` (a `K'`-vertex). - `K'-carrier(midpoint(ab)) = {p_{n/2}} = {midpoint(ab)}` (also a `K'`-vertex).
REPORT_LITERAL.md:95 advice Counterexample to a "K'-carrier nesting" formulation we tried :: These are **not nested**: neither `{a} ‚äÜ {midpoint(ab)}` nor vice versa.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:97 advice Counterexample to a "K'-carrier nesting" formulation we tried :: So `Sd`-vertex `K'-carriers` are not nested in general.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:101 advice Concrete downstream uses requiring LITERAL refinement :: The literal refinement theorem is required to prove **Moise's Theorem 1** (common subdivision exists), which is then used heavily for **piecewise-linear (PL) homeomorphism transport** arguments. Specifically:
REPORT_LITERAL.md:105 advice Use 1: Theorem 1 (common subdivision) ‚Äî internal use :: ```isabelle theorem Theorem_GT_1: fixes K L1 L2 :: "'a::euclidean_space set set" assumes hKfin: "finite K" assumes hL1: "geotop_is_subdivision L1 K" assumes hL2: "geotop_is_subdivision L2 K" shows "‚àÉL. geotop_is_subdivision L L1 ‚àß geotop_is_subdivision L L2" ```
REPORT_LITERAL.md:114 advice Use 1: Theorem 1 (common subdivision) ‚Äî internal use :: Proof outline (in our formalization): - Apply literal refinement to get `m, n` with `Sd·µê K` a subdivision of `L1`, `Sd‚Åø K` a subdivision of `L2`. - Take `N = max m n`. By monotonicity (`Sd` preserves subdivision-of relation), `Sd·¥∫ K` is a subdivision of `Sd·µê K` and of `Sd‚Åø K`, hence (transitivity) of `L1` and `L2`.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:118 advice Use 1: Theorem 1 (common subdivision) ‚Äî internal use :: The literal refinement is the only nontrivial input.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:122 advice Use 2: PL-isomorphism transport :: Given a PL homeomorphism `f : |L| ‚Üí |K|` where `L‚ÇÅ` is a subdivision of `L`, we want to construct a subdivision `L‚ÇÉ` of `L‚ÇÅ` such that `f` is simplicial on each simplex of `L‚ÇÉ` (refining the original PL structure). Sketch from `b/GeoTopBase.thy` line 4098:
REPORT_LITERAL.md:124 advice Use 2: PL-isomorphism transport :: ```isabelle (* (4) Apply Theorem_GT_1 to get a common subdivision K_3 of fL_1 and K_1 (uses finite K hypothesis). *) obtain K‚ÇÉ where hK‚ÇÉ_fL‚ÇÅ: "geotop_is_subdivision K‚ÇÉ fL‚ÇÅ" and hK‚ÇÉ_K‚ÇÅ: "geotop_is_subdivision K‚ÇÉ K‚ÇÅ" using Theorem_GT_1[OF hKfin hfL‚ÇÅ_sub hK‚ÇÅK] by ...
REPORT_LITERAL.md:131 advice Use 2: PL-isomorphism transport :: (* (5) Pull K_3 back through f to get L_3 < L_1 with f : L_3 ‚âÖ K_3. *) define L‚ÇÉ where "L‚ÇÉ = (ŒªœÉ. inv_into (geotop_polyhedron L) f ` œÉ) ` K‚ÇÉ" ```
REPORT_LITERAL.md:135 advice Use 2: PL-isomorphism transport :: This step requires that for each `œÉ ‚àà K‚ÇÉ` we have `œÉ ‚äÜ œÑ` for some `œÑ ‚àà K‚ÇÅ` **literally**, so that `f‚Åª¬π(œÉ) ‚äÜ f‚Åª¬π(œÑ)` lands inside a single simplex of `L` where `f‚Åª¬π` is well-defined and PL.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:137 advice Use 2: PL-isomorphism transport :: If we only had simplicial-approximation-style refinement (`œÉ` mapped to some `K‚ÇÅ`-simplex via a simplicial approximation map), then `f‚Åª¬π(œÉ)` would not directly land in a single `L`-simplex, breaking the construction.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:141 advice Use 3: Repeated PL-isomorphism transport for ¬ß¬ß2-3 of Moise :: The PL transport argument (Use 2) is the workhorse for many ¬ß¬ß2-3 theorems (Moise's text). It is invoked tens of times. Each invocation requires literal refinement (set-containment) for the inverse-image construction to be well-defined.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:145 advice Summary of downstream use :: Without literal refinement: - `Theorem_GT_1` cannot be proved. - All ¬ß¬ß2-3 PL-isomorphism transport arguments fail. - The ¬ß1 (Theorem 1) Moise statement is unproven.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:152 advice Specific question for a mathematician :: We need a classical proof of the following statement:
REPORT_LITERAL.md:154 advice Specific question for a mathematician :: > **Theorem (LITERAL Sd refinement):** Let `K` be a finite Euclidean simplicial complex in ‚Ñù‚Åø. Let `K'` be a subdivision of `K`. Then there exists `N ‚àà ‚Ñï` such that for every simplex `œÑ ‚àà Sd·¥∫ K`, there is some simplex `œÉ' ‚àà K'` with `œÑ ‚äÜ œÉ'` (set-containment).
REPORT_LITERAL.md:156 advice Specific question for a mathematician :: Equivalently: `Sd·¥∫ K` is a subdivision of `K'` (in Munkres' sense, Definition 15.1).
REPORT_LITERAL.md:160 advice What we have :: 1. Mesh shrinkage: `Sd·¥∫ K` has all simplices of arbitrarily small diameter as `N ‚Üí ‚àû`. 2. Lebesgue number for the cover of `|K| = |K'|` by `K'`-vertex open stars: there's `Œª > 0` such that every set of diameter `< Œª` lies in some open star `St(v, K')`. 3. Combining 1+2: for `N` large, every `œÑ ‚àà Sd·¥∫ K` lies in some `St(v, K')` for some `K'`-vertex `v`. 4. Munkres' Lemma 14.4 / iff-form: For any non-empty finite `V ‚äÜ |K'|`, `‚à©_{w ‚àà V} St(w, K') ‚âÝ ‚àÖ` iff there's some `K'`-simplex containing all of `V`.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:167 advice What we need :: A direct proof that `œÑ ‚äÜ œÉ'` for some `œÉ' ‚àà K'`, NOT just `œÑ ‚äÜ St(v, K')`.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:171 advice Possible approaches we've considered (and their issues) :: **Approach A (per-point rel-distance):** failed counterexample above.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:173 advice Possible approaches we've considered (and their issues) :: **Approach B (`K'`-carrier nesting):** failed counterexample above.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:175 advice Possible approaches we've considered (and their issues) :: **Approach C (Sd-vertex barycentric structure):** the `Sd`-vertices are barycenters of `K`-flag chains. Possibly the chain structure forces `K'`-carriers into a specific configuration. We have not been able to make this precise.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:177 advice Possible approaches we've considered (and their issues) :: **Approach D (apply Munkres' Theorem 16.1 simplicial approximation, then refine):** the simplicial approximation `f : Sd·¥∫ K ‚Üí K'` provides set-membership in stars, but not literal set-containment of simplices.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:181 advice What we'd like :: Either: - **(I)** A clean classical proof of the LITERAL refinement statement (perhaps in Spanier *Algebraic Topology*, Hatcher *Algebraic Topology*, or another reference we haven't checked). - **(II)** Confirmation that the LITERAL refinement is genuinely classical folklore that requires careful elementary geometry of barycentric subdivisions, and a sketch of the precise argument. - **(III)** Counterexample showing that the LITERAL refinement is FALSE in general, so that our formalization needs to be restructured to use a weaker simplicial-approximation-style notion of subdivision.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:186 advice What we'd like :: Outcome (III) would be unexpected ‚Äî we believe LITERAL refinement is true, since standard textbooks treat "common subdivision" as routine ‚Äî but we cannot rule it out.
REPORT_LITERAL.md:190 advice Current state of formalization :: - The basis (`b0/GeoTopBase0.thy` + `b/GeoTopBase.thy`) has **1 sorry** on the LITERAL refinement claim, structured into a 6-step Isar proof: - Steps 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 are FULLY PROVEN using existing infrastructure. - Step 4 (the deep analytic step) is a single `sorry` on: > `(‚ãÇ_{w ‚àà V_œÑ} open_star(w, K')) ‚âÝ ‚àÖ` where `V_œÑ` is the vertex set of an `Sd·µê K` simplex `œÑ ‚äÜ open_star(v, K')` for some `K'`-vertex `v` (the Lebesgue conclusion).

REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:10 advice REPORT ‚Äî Theorem_GT_1 in Isabelle/HOL: a sorry-free proof :: ```isabelle theorem Theorem_GT_1: fixes K L1 L2 :: "'a::euclidean_space set set" assumes hKfin: "finite K" assumes hL1: "geotop_is_subdivision L1 K" assumes hL2: "geotop_is_subdivision L2 K" shows "‚àÉL. geotop_is_subdivision L L1 ‚àß geotop_is_subdivision L L2" ```

REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:22 advice REPORT ‚Äî Theorem_GT_1 in Isabelle/HOL: a sorry-free proof :: The story is essentially a worked example of a classical PL-topology argument done from scratch on top of HOL-Analysis. It also includes a real research moment: the proof attempt we *started* with turned out to be founded on a **false** auxiliary lemma.
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:27 advice REPORT ‚Äî Theorem_GT_1 in Isabelle/HOL: a sorry-free proof :: ---
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:31 advice 1. What Moise actually says :: Moise's text (`geotop.tex` here) is, in this part of the book, a survey chapter. Theorem 1 is *stated* on p. 7 without proof. The general common-subdivision claim never gets a direct proof in the chapter; instead, Moise defers the work to the problem set, where it is broken up by dimension:
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:36 advice 1. What Moise actually says :: | Problem | Statement | |--------|-----------| | 0.18 | Let `K` be a finite complex in `R¬≤`, and `{L_i}` a finite collection of lines. Then `K` has a subdivision `K_1` in which each `L_i ‚à© |K|` forms a subcomplex. | | **0.19** | **Every two subdivisions `K_1, K_2` of a 2-simplex `œÉ¬≤ ‚äÇ R¬≤` have a common subdivision.** | | **0.20** | **Let `K` be a 2-dimensional complex. Then every two subdivisions of `K` have a common subdivision.** | | 0.26 | (R¬≥ analogue of 0.18 with planes.) | | **0.27** | **Every two subdivisions of a 3-simplex have a common subdivision.** | | **0.28** | **Let `K` be a 3-dimensional complex. Then every two subdivisions of `K` have a common subdivision.** |
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:45 advice 1. What Moise actually says :: The relevant tools Moise gives the reader for these problems are the inductive definition of the (first) barycentric subdivision `bK` (p. 35-ish in our text, line 1197 in `geotop.tex`) plus the three facts numbered there:
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:49 advice 1. What Moise actually says :: > 5. For each `K`, `bK` is a complex. > 6. `bK` is a subdivision of `K`. > 7. `‚Äñb œÉ‚Åø‚Äñ ‚â§ ¬Ω ‚ÄñœÉ‚Åø‚Äñ`, for each `œÉ‚Åø`.
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:53 advice 1. What Moise actually says :: So the *spirit* of the textbook approach to common subdivision in arbitrary dimension is "iterate `b` until things fit"; the *letter* of the textbook proof is left to the reader. Our own writeup of this folkloric argument lives in `early.tex ¬ß4` and was the original target of the formalization.
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:60 advice 2. The original (failed) approach: iterated barycentric subdivision :: The plan we tried to formalize was the standard one. Given a complex `K` and a subdivision `K'` of `K`, we wanted to show that *some* iterated barycentric subdivision `Sd^N K` refines `K'`, i.e. every simplex of `Sd^N K` is contained in some simplex of `K'`. Once we had that, a common subdivision of two subdivisions `L1, L2` of `K` falls out: take `N = max(m, n)` where `Sd^m K` refines `L1` and `Sd^n K` refines `L2`, and use transitivity of subdivision.
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:68 advice 2. The original (failed) approach: iterated barycentric subdivision :: This is the path the formalization originally followed. The auxiliary step became, in Isabelle, the lemma
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:71 advice 2. The original (failed) approach: iterated barycentric subdivision :: ```isabelle geotop_iterated_Sd_refines_subdivision: finite K ‚üπ geotop_is_subdivision K' K ‚üπ ‚àÉm. geotop_is_subdivision (geotop_iterated_Sd m K) K' ```
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:77 advice 2. The original (failed) approach: iterated barycentric subdivision :: The proof we tried to give was the standard Lebesgue-number argument on the finite open-star cover of `|K|` by vertex stars of `K'`, combined with `geotop_iterated_Sd_mesh` (mesh shrinks by factor `n / (n+1)` per iteration).
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:81 advice 2. The original (failed) approach: iterated barycentric subdivision :: It failed. The structural sub-lemmas were fine, but the analytic step `Step 4` ‚Äî "for every simplex `œÑ` of a sufficiently fine `Sd^N K`, the open stars of `œÑ`'s vertices in `K'` have non-empty intersection" ‚Äî refused to go through. We sat on a `sorry` at that step and gradually realized the lemma surrounding the sorry was just *false*.
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:89 advice 2.1 The 1-dimensional counterexample :: The cleanest disproof is one-dimensional. Take
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:91 advice 2.1 The 1-dimensional counterexample :: * `K = {[0,1], {0}, {1}}` ‚Äî a single 1-simplex with its two vertex faces. * `K' = {[0, 1/3], [1/3, 1], {0}, {1/3}, {1}}` ‚Äî the subdivision of `[0,1]` at the point `1/3`.
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:95 advice 2.1 The 1-dimensional counterexample :: Iterating barycentric subdivision on `K` only ever produces vertices at *dyadic* rationals: `m / 2^k` for various `m, k`. The point `1/3` is not dyadic, so for every `N` there is exactly one sub-interval of `Sd^N K` whose interior contains `1/3`. That sub-interval is contained in **neither** `[0, 1/3]` nor `[1/3, 1]`. Hence `Sd^N K` never refines `K'` literally, contradicting the lemma we were trying to prove.
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:104 advice 2.2 Why the folkloric argument fails :: Our writeup in `early.tex ¬ß4.5` had a subtle bug. The argument went:
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:106 advice 2.2 Why the folkloric argument fails :: > Choose `m` so each simplex `œÑ` of `Sd^m(K)` has diameter `< Œ¥`. Then `œÑ` is > contained in some open star `st_{K'}(v)`. Pick `x ‚àà int(œÑ)`. Since > `œÑ ‚äÇ st_{K'}(v)`, the point `x` lies in `int(œÉ)` for some `œÉ ‚àà K'` having > `v` as a vertex. Both `œÑ` and `œÉ` lie in some simplex of `K`. Inside that > ambient simplex, `x ‚àà int(œÑ) ‚à© int(œÉ)`; since interiors of distinct > simplices in a simplicial complex are disjoint, `œÑ ‚äÇ œÉ`.
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:113 advice 2.2 Why the folkloric argument fails :: The mistake is in the last sentence: "interiors of distinct simplices in a simplicial complex are disjoint" is true *within one* complex, but `œÑ` and `œÉ` belong to **different** complexes (`œÑ ‚àà Sd^m K`, `œÉ ‚àà K'`). They both subdivide `K`, but the disjointness fact does not transfer across two subdivisions of the same complex.
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:119 advice 2.2 Why the folkloric argument fails :: In the dyadic counterexample above (with `m = 1`, `Sd K = K|_{1/2}`), the simplex `œÑ = [0, 1/2]` has interior `(0, 1/2)`, which intersects both the interior `(0, 1/3)` of `[0,1/3] ‚àà K'` and the interior `(1/3, 1)` of `[1/3, 1] ‚àà K'`, so `œÑ` is contained in neither. The Lebesgue argument correctly places `œÑ` *in some open star* of `K'`, but cannot place it inside any single closed simplex of `K'`.
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:126 advice 2.2 Why the folkloric argument fails :: So iterated barycentric subdivision does give a refinement of the *non-literal* / "Munkres-type-with-closure" sort, but cannot give a literal-containment refinement, which is what `geotop_is_subdivision` demands. We needed a different machine.
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:131 advice 2.2 Why the folkloric argument fails :: We asked a mathematician (`ANSWER_REPORT.md`) and they pointed us at Hudson's *Piecewise Linear Topology* (Chapter I, ¬ß2-3) and the **overlay cell complex** construction. That became PLAN3.
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:135 advice 2.2 Why the folkloric argument fails :: ---
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:138 advice 3. The replacement strategy: overlay cell complex + order-complex :: triangulation
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:140 advice 3. The replacement strategy: overlay cell complex + order-complex :: The classical PL fix factors through *cells* (convex polytopes) rather than simplices. The high-level recipe:
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:143 advice 3. The replacement strategy: overlay cell complex + order-complex :: 1. Start with two simplicial complexes `L1, L2` with `|L1| = |L2|`. 2. Form pairwise intersections `œÉ ‚à© œÑ` for `œÉ ‚àà L1, œÑ ‚àà L2`. These are non-empty convex polytopes ‚Äî *cells*. Close under faces. Call the result `C = overlay_complex L1 L2`. It is a finite *cell* complex (Hudson's notion). 3. Triangulate `C` simplicially with no new vertices, getting a finite simplicial complex `M`. (Hudson Lemma 1.4 / Munkres ¬ß17 ‚Äî barycentric subdivision of the cell poset.) `M` literally refines `C`. 4. Because every cell of `C` lies inside a simplex of `L1` *and* inside a simplex of `L2`, `M` literally refines both `L1` and `L2`.
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:154 advice 3. The replacement strategy: overlay cell complex + order-complex :: The point that makes step 4 *literal* (and dodges the dyadic obstruction above) is that `C` introduces new vertices wherever the boundaries of `L1` and `L2` cross. With `K = [0,1]` and `K' = K |_{1/3}` the overlay just contains `1/3` as a 0-cell, and the order-complex triangulation has `1/3` as an honest vertex. No analytic limit argument is required.
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:160 advice 3. The replacement strategy: overlay cell complex + order-complex :: ---
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:164 advice 4. The 8-phase formalization (PLAN3) :: We split the work into eight phases, each with a `PLAN3_PHASE_n.md` plan file. The first commit landed `2026-04-26`-ish (commit `9533507c`); Phase 8 landed at `d5336274`. **907 commits total** in the repository, of which the plan-3 family contributes the bulk of the geotop-formalization activity.

REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:183 advice 4. The 8-phase formalization (PLAN3) :: Phases 1-4 are mostly bookkeeping on top of HOL-Analysis polytope theory (`Polytope.thy`, `Convex.thy`). Phases 5-8 are where the substantial mathematics lives.
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:189 advice 4.1 Phase 5: order-complex triangulation (the hard core) :: Phase 5 says: *every finite cell complex `C` has a simplicial subdivision*. The witness is the **order complex**: the simplicial complex whose simplices are the convex hulls of barycenters of strictly-increasing chains of cells in `C`. This is the workhorse of barycentric subdivision when the underlying object is a cell complex rather than just a simplicial complex. Munkres does the simplicial special case in ¬ß15-17; Hudson does the general cell case as Lemma 1.4.
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:197 advice 4.1 Phase 5: order-complex triangulation (the hard core) :: The simplicial-complex axioms are:
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:199 advice 4.1 Phase 5: order-complex triangulation (the hard core) :: * **K.0** every element is a (Moise) simplex, * **K.1** closed under faces, * **K.2** pairwise intersections are empty or a common face, * **K.3** locally finite.
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:204 advice 4.1 Phase 5: order-complex triangulation (the hard core) :: Phase 5 ended up with an internal sub-numbering for clarity:
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:206 advice 4.1 Phase 5: order-complex triangulation (the hard core) :: | Sub-step | Lemma | Lines | Idea | |----------|-------|-------|------| | 5.1-5.5 | definitions (cell barycenter, cell-flag, chain-simplex, order complex) | ~60 | | | 5.3 main | cell-flag barycenters are affinely independent | ~80 | rev_induct + `affine_hull_face_of_disjoint_rel_interior` + `affine_independent_insert` | | 5.6a (K.0) | every chain-simplex is a Moise simplex | ~30 | uses 5.3 main + `geotop_ai_imp_general_position` | | 5.6c (K.3) | order complex is finite | ~30 | bound length of cell-flags by `card C`, use `finite_lists_length_le` | | 5.6b (K.1) | order complex closed under faces | ~80 | `simplex_vertices_unique` + filter sub-flag | | **carrier**| every `x ‚àà ‚ÄñC‚Äñ` has unique ...
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:231 advice 4.1 Phase 5: order-complex triangulation (the hard core) :: K.2 was, by a wide margin, the hardest step. The na√Øve proof attempt ‚Äî try to show `V_{c1} ‚à™ V_{c2}` is affinely independent in general ‚Äî *fails*, because V_{c1} ‚à™ V_{c2}* really can be affinely dependent (e.g. four barycenter points in the plane from incompatible flags through a common 2-cell). The right proof goes through the carrier-cell uniqueness argument:
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:237 advice 4.1 Phase 5: order-complex triangulation (the hard core) :: 1. For any `x ‚àà chain_simplex(c)`, the maximum cell in `c` whose barycenter has positive weight in the unique convex combination of `x` is exactly the carrier of `x` (Lemma 5.6d.b combined with carrier uniqueness from the cell-complex structure). 2. For `x ‚àà œÉ_c1 ‚à© œÉ_c2`, both carriers in the two flags coincide (Lemma 5.6d.c). 3. The `(u, y)` in the decomposition `x = u¬∑b(A) + (1-u)¬∑y` with `y ‚àà rel_frontier(A)` is uniquely determined by `x` and `A` (Lemma 5.6d.f, the *radial decomposition uniqueness* lemma). 4. So in case 3b of K.2 (where the top of both flags equals the carrier of `x`), the two decompositions in `c1` and `c2` produce the *same* `y`, `y ‚àà chain_simplex(butlast c1) ‚à© chai...
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:251 advice 4.1 Phase 5: order-complex triangulation (the hard core) :: The radial-uniqueness Lemma 5.6d.f is the conceptual heart of K.2. It says:
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:253 advice 4.1 Phase 5: order-complex triangulation (the hard core) :: > If `x` is in the relative interior of a convex set `S` and we write > `x = u¬∑b + (1-u)¬∑y = u‚Ä≤¬∑b + (1-u‚Ä≤)¬∑y‚Ä≤` with `b ‚àà rel_interior S`, > `y, y‚Ä≤ ‚àà rel_frontier S`, and `u, u‚Ä≤ ‚àà [0, 1)`, then `u = u‚Ä≤` and > `y = y‚Ä≤`.
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:258 advice 4.1 Phase 5: order-complex triangulation (the hard core) :: The proof: any two `rel_frontier` points on the same open ray from `b` must coincide, because otherwise the closer one would lie inside the open segment to the farther one, and that open segment is contained in `rel_interior S` (the lemma `rel_interior_closure_convex_segment` in HOL-Analysis), which contradicts the closer point being in `rel_frontier`.
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:266 advice 4.2 Phase 6: bridging back to simplicial subdivisions :: The general bridge is a clean 80-line argument:
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:268 advice 4.2 Phase 6: bridging back to simplicial subdivisions :: ```isabelle lemma geotop_simplicial_subdivision_from_cell_subdivision: fixes M C L :: "'a::euclidean_space set set" assumes "geotop_is_complex L" assumes "geotop_is_complex M" assumes "geotop_cell_is_subdivision M C" assumes "‚àÄA ‚àà C. ‚àÉœÉ ‚àà L. A ‚äÜ œÉ" assumes "‚àÄœÉ ‚àà L. œÉ = ‚ãÉ {A ‚àà C. A ‚äÜ œÉ}" shows "geotop_is_subdivision M L" ```
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:279 advice 4.2 Phase 6: bridging back to simplicial subdivisions :: The applied versions for the overlay scenario fall straight out: the hypothesis `‚àÄA ‚àà C. ‚àÉœÉ ‚àà L1. A ‚äÜ œÉ` is `geotop_overlay_complex_refines_left` from Phase 4, and `œÉ = ‚ãÉ {A ‚àà overlay_complex. A ‚äÜ œÉ}` is a small new lemma that proves *any* `x ‚àà œÉ ‚àà L1` lies in the overlay cell `œÉ ‚à© œÑ` for some `œÑ ‚àà L2` containing `x` (existence of `œÑ` follows from `|L1| = |L2|`).
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:287 advice 4.3 Phase 7: the common-subdivision theorem :: ```isabelle theorem geotop_common_subdivision_finite: fixes L1 L2 :: "'a::euclidean_space set set" assumes hL1: "geotop_is_complex L1" assumes hL2: "geotop_is_complex L2" assumes hL1fin: "finite L1" assumes hL2fin: "finite L2" assumes hpoly: "geotop_polyhedron L1 = geotop_polyhedron L2" shows "‚àÉM. geotop_is_subdivision M L1 ‚àß geotop_is_subdivision M L2" ```
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:298 advice 4.3 Phase 7: the common-subdivision theorem :: The proof is now mechanical assembly:
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:300 advice 4.3 Phase 7: the common-subdivision theorem :: 1. `C = overlay_complex L1 L2` is a cell complex (Phase 4). 2. `M = order_complex C` is a simplicial cell-subdivision of `C` (Phase 5, theorem `geotop_cell_complex_has_simplicial_subdivision`). 3. `M` is a simplicial subdivision of `L1` (Phase 6.2). 4. `M` is a simplicial subdivision of `L2` (Phase 6.3).

REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:345 advice 5.1 The `by100` discipline :: Per `CLAUDE.md`, every `by` step uses the `by100` wrapper which times out the inner method at 100 ms. The motivation is simple: in a file this long (`14 886` lines, `262` declarations), an unguarded `auto`/`blast` can blow through the build budget on a single bad simp call. The 100 ms cap forces proofs to decompose.
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:351 advice 5.1 The `by100` discipline :: It also forced an interesting workflow: after a single big proof addition we would routinely see other, *pre-existing* `by100` calls start to flake under the new memory pressure. The fix was timing-driven decomposition: dump per-line timings with
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:356 advice 5.1 The `by100` discipline :: ```bash isabelle eval_at -t -d . -l Top0 b0/GeoTopBase0.thy <last-line> > timing.txt ```
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:360 advice 5.1 The `by100` discipline :: then attack any line over `~0.06s` first. Two big wins:
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:362 advice 5.1 The `by100` discipline :: * Replace `using lemma_name[OF X] by (by100 simp)` with the direct `by (rule lemma_name[OF X])` whenever the lemma is an iff/equality. 16 sites of `convex_hull_finite[OF X]` flipped this way alone. * Replace `by (by100 simp)` with `by (by100 metis)` on chains of equality substitutions where simp's normalisation is doing a lot of unnecessary work.
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:369 advice 5.1 The `by100` discipline :: After this pass the build dropped from "5‚Äì10 retries" to "1‚Äì2 retries" under typical load.
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:374 advice 5.2 Choice of barycenter :: `geotop_cell_barycenter` is defined as `SOME b. b ‚àà rel_interior C`, *not* "centroid of vertices". This makes the affine independence proof clean (we don't need to manage an explicit vertex set) and matches what HOL-Analysis's lemmas expect. The cost is that we never compute concrete coordinates ‚Äî which in fairness was never the goal.
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:382 advice 5.3 What we got from HOL-Analysis :: The proof would not have been feasible in the time budget without HOL-Analysis's `Polytope.thy` and friends. Critical imports:
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:385 advice 5.3 What we got from HOL-Analysis :: * `affine_hull_face_of_disjoint_rel_interior` (proper face's affine hull misses rel_interior) ‚Äî used pervasively. * `face_of_convex_hull_affine_independent` (HOL face_of ‚Üî conv hull of vertex subset, when AI) ‚Äî the bridge from K.2 in the simplicial sense to the cell-complex face notion. * `rel_interior_closure_convex_segment` (open segment from rel_interior to closure stays in rel_interior) ‚Äî the heart of the radial-uniqueness argument and the Stripping lemma. * `segment_to_rel_frontier` (existence of a rel_frontier point along any ray) ‚Äî the engine of Phase 5.7 ‚äá. * `convex_hull_insert` (explicit decomposition) ‚Äî used in 5.6d.b, 5.6d.d, 5.6d main. * `extreme_point_of_convex_hull_affine_i...
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:402 advice 5.4 What turned out to be subtle :: The single most painful phase, both intellectually and in lines, was K.2. A small list of things that *looked* like they should work and didn't:
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:405 advice 5.4 What turned out to be subtle :: * "Take a common refinement of the two flags." Doesn't exist when the flags are incompatible (e.g. `c1 = [{a}, e_{ab}, T]`, `c2 = [{a}, e_{ac}, T]`). * "Use AI of `V_{c1} ‚à™ V_{c2}`." False in general: the example above is four AI-barred points in the plane. * "Show that `u_1 = u_2` is automatic from the bary-coord uniqueness on a single AI set." False ‚Äî bary-coord uniqueness is *per* AI set, and the two flags use different ones.
REPORT_Theorem_GT_1.md:414 advice 5.4 What turned out to be subtle :: The trick that finally worked is the combination of Stripping (5.6d.d) for case 3a and *radial decomposition uniqueness* (5.6d.f) for case 3b. The latter never actually mentions barycentric coordinates: it lives entirely inside `rel_interior(carrier(x))`, treats the convex set as opaque, and uses only the geometric fact that two `rel_frontier` points on the same open ray must coincide.

STATUS_REPORT.md:104 advice Tactical lessons learned (cached helper patterns) :: - **`by simp` on subspace topology rewrites** can loop. Use `subst (1 2)` for controlled rewriting.
STATUS_REPORT.md:107 advice Tactical lessons learned (cached helper patterns) :: - **The bridge pattern works fine when constructed inline** (build green with full proof of `homeomorphism UNIV UNIV h (inv h)`). The block is purely on RULE APPLICATION via `[OF ...]`, not on the construction.
STATUS_REPORT.md:111 advice Tactical lessons learned (cached helper patterns) :: - **HOL-Analysis name shadowing**: `closedin_Inter` exists in both `HOL-Analysis.Abstract_Topology` and `Top1_Ch2`. Use qualified `Top1_Ch2.closedin_Inter` to disambiguate.
instructions.md:1 advice advice :: To configure your repository for faster incremental builds using Isabelle sessions, follow these steps. This setup separates the initial chapters into a base session (Top0) that Isabelle can cache, so that changes in later chapters (Top1) do not trigger a full rebuild of the entire project.
instructions.md:4 advice advice :: 1. Reorganize the Directory Structure Create a subdirectory named i inside tst and move the first three chapter files into it:
instructions.md:7 advice advice :: 1 mkdir -p tst/i 2 mv tst/Top1_Ch2.thy tst/i/ 3 mv tst/Top1_Ch3.thy tst/i/ 4 mv tst/Top1_Ch4.thy tst/i/
instructions.md:12 advice advice :: 2. Update Theory Imports Modify the import statement in tst/Top1_Ch5_8.thy to point to the Top1_Ch4 theory within the new Top0 session:
instructions.md:15 advice advice :: File: tst/Top1_Ch5_8.thy Change:
instructions.md:18 advice advice :: 1 theory Top1_Ch5_8 2 imports Top1_Ch4 To: 1 theory Top1_Ch5_8 2 imports "Top0.Top1_Ch4"
instructions.md:24 advice advice :: 3. Configure the Session ROOT File Replace the content of tst/ROOT with the following configuration. This defines the two sessions and specifies their relationship:
instructions.md:27 advice advice :: File: tst/ROOT
instructions.md:29 advice advice :: 1 session Top0 in i = HOL + 2 options [quick_and_dirty] 3 theories 4 Top1_Ch2 5 Top1_Ch3 6 Top1_Ch4 7 8 session Top1 = Top0 + 9 options [quick_and_dirty, timeout = 120] 10 theories 11 Top1_Ch5_8 12 Top1
instructions.md:42 advice advice :: 4. Build and Verify Run the build command from the tst directory. Isabelle will now build Top0 once and cache it. Subsequent builds will only process the theories in Top1 unless you modify files in Top0.
instructions.md:46 advice advice :: 1 cd tst 2 ../isabelle/bin/isabelle build -D .
instructions.md:49 advice advice :: 5. Using CLI Tools with Sessions When using auxiliary tools like eval_at or process_theories, you must now specify the logic session (-l Top0) and include the current directory in the search path (-d .):
instructions.md:53 advice advice :: * To evaluate a line in Chapters 2-4:
instructions.md:55 advice advice :: 1 ../isabelle/bin/isabelle eval_at -d . -l Top0 i/Top1_Ch2.thy <LINE_NUMBER> * To evaluate a line in Chapters 5-8 or the main Top1 theory:
instructions.md:58 advice advice :: 1 ../isabelle/bin/isabelle eval_at -d . -l Top1 Top1_Ch5_8.thy <LINE_NUMBER>
